Summary:- IPC Section 151 deals with situations where an individual is found guilty of joining an unlawful assembly or taking part in a public disturbance with the intention of committing an offence.
example_scenarios: A group gathers with the declared intention of vandalizing a government building, but are arrested before any damage occurs.
Individuals assemble with weapons, openly stating their plan to attack a rival community, but are dispersed by police before violence erupts.

title: Assaulting or Obstructing Public Servant with Deadly Weapon

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes assaulting or obstructing a public servant while armed with a deadly weapon during discharge of official duties.
section_text:
Section 152 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of assaulting or obstructing a public servant while performing their duty. Specifically, it addresses the situation where an individual uses force or violence against a public servant while they are discharging their official responsibilities.

Summary:- This section aims to punish those who attack or obstruct public servants, such as police officers or government officials, in the execution of their duties. It acts as a safeguard for public officials against such interference while carrying out their lawful work.
example_scenarios: A person threatens a tax inspector with a knife while the inspector is conducting a raid on a business premises.
During an eviction drive, an individual wields an iron rod to prevent police officers from carrying out court orders.

title: Promoting Enmity Between Different Groups
description: Punishes promoting enmity between groups based on religion, race, place of birth, etc., and acts that may incite violence or hostility.
section_text:
Section 153 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for promoting enmity between different groups on grounds of religion, race, place of birth, residence, language, etc., and doing acts that may incite violence or hostility.

Summary:- This section aims to prevent actions that could incite hatred or enmity between groups, causing potential harm to public peace. It criminalizes the act of promoting discord and hostility between different communities.
example_scenarios: A political leader gives a speech deliberately vilifying a particular religious community and encouraging violence against them.
Social media posts are circulated with false information designed to create tension between two ethnic groups in a mixed neighborhood.

title: Procedure for Recording FIR in Cognizable Cases

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Affecting The Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency And Morals.

description: Outlines the procedure for registering First Information Report (FIR) for cognizable offences and police obligations.
section_text:
This section pertains to the information related to the commission of a cognizable offense.

Summary:- Section 154 deals with the requirement to report a cognizable offense to the police. It mandates that when a person informs the police about an offense that is cognizable, the police must record it in writing. This recorded information is referred to as an FIR (First Information Report). The police are obligated to act on it and investigate the matter further.
legal_elements: Police duty to record in writing
example_scenarios: A victim of theft goes to the police station to report the crime, and the officer is legally required to record the details in an FIR.
Witnesses to a robbery provide information to police, who must document it as an FIR before beginning investigation.

title: Questioning of Woman's Character in Sexual Offence Cases
description: Provides procedural safeguards regarding questioning of a woman's character in cases involving sexual offences.
section_text:
Section 155 deals with "Accused to be questioned" in cases of an offence against women, specifically regarding their character. It allows the questioning of a woman's character only if the offence involves her sexual conduct or moral character.

Summary:- This section provides a provision regarding the questioning of a woman's character in cases where it is pertinent to the charge being framed, such as those involving sexual offences. This questioning can only happen under specific circumstances, ensuring protection for the woman against unnecessary character attacks.
example_scenarios: During a rape trial, the defense seeks to question the victim about her past sexual history, but the court limits such questioning to what is directly relevant to the case.
In a sexual harassment case, the accused attempts to introduce evidence about the complainant's character, which is carefully scrutinized by the judge for relevance.

title: Police Power to Investigate Without Magistrate Permission

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating To Documents And Property Marks

description: Empowers police to investigate cognizable offences without prior permission from a magistrate.
section_text:
Section 156 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the Power of police to investigate without the permission of a magistrate in certain cases, as specified under the law.

Summary:- This section empowers police officers to investigate an offence without obtaining prior permission from a magistrate. The section gives the police the authority to initiate an investigation when a cognizable offence is suspected, which means the police can arrest without a warrant and start their investigation.
example_scenarios: Police begin investigating a robbery immediately after receiving the FIR without waiting for court approval.
Upon witnessing a serious assault, police officers start collecting evidence and questioning witnesses without magistrate authorization.

title: Duty to Arrest Person Who Escaped from Custody

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Directs police to apprehend accused persons who have escaped from lawful custody or are evading arrest for cognizable offences.
section_text:
This section outlines the duty of a police officer to arrest a person who has committed a cognizable offence if the person is found to be at large and has escaped from lawful custody or should be arrested for further investigation. It helps maintain the accountability of individuals suspected of serious crimes, ensuring that the law enforcement authorities continue their work without delay.

Summary:- Section 157 of the Indian Penal Code directs a police officer to take immediate action to apprehend an accused person who has committed a cognizable offence, particularly in cases where the person has fled from lawful custody or is evading arrest. The section is designed to aid in the swift execution of the law when necessary. It empowers police officers to act efficiently in cases where public safety or the criminal justice system is at risk due to someone's evasion.
example_scenarios: Police must actively search for and arrest a suspect who escaped from police custody while being transported to court.
Officers have a duty to apprehend an accused person who has been avoiding arrest after a non-bailable warrant was issued.

title: Possession or Disposal of Stolen Property
description: Punishes receiving, possessing, or disposing of stolen property with knowledge that it was stolen.
section_text:
Section 158 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of the person who, while residing in a town or place, receives, possesses, or disposes of stolen property with the knowledge that it has been stolen.

Summary:- This section penalizes individuals who are found in possession of stolen goods or property, knowing that the goods have been stolen, and the person is residing in the town or place where the property was found.
legal_elements: Residing in town where property found
example_scenarios: A pawn shop owner knowingly accepts and sells jewelry that was recently stolen from a nearby residence.
An individual is found with a large quantity of electronic goods that were stolen in a recent warehouse burglary, with evidence showing they knew the items were stolen.

title: Affray (Fighting in Public Place)
description: Defines and punishes affray - fighting between two or more persons in public that disturbs public peace.
section_text:
Section 159 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of affray. It addresses situations where two or more people disturb public peace by fighting in public places.

Summary:- The section defines "affray" as an instance where two or more individuals fight in a manner that causes fear or disturbance to the public. This fighting doesn't need to cause actual harm but should be of such a nature that people in the vicinity feel threatened or disturbed.
example_scenarios: Two individuals engage in a physical fight in a crowded market, causing shoppers to flee and disrupting business activities.
A group of people brawl outside a bar late at night, creating noise and causing fear among residents in the area.

title: Punishment for Affray
description: Specifies punishment for engaging in affray or unlawful assembly that disturbs public peace.
section_text:
This section deals with punishment for engaging in a public nuisance by committing acts of rioting or unlawful assembly, particularly when done in a manner that disturbs public peace.

Summary:- Section 160 of the Indian Penal Code specifies the punishment for individuals who create a public nuisance by disturbing the peace and order of the community. The section specifically addresses actions where the accused engages in riots, violent behavior, or activities that could disrupt public tranquility.
example_scenarios: Individuals involved in a violent fight in a public park are arrested and charged under this section.
Participants in a street brawl that caused traffic disruption and public fear face prosecution.

title: Bribery of Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences By Or Relating To Public Servants

description: Punishes bribery involving public servants - both giving and accepting bribes for official acts.
section_text:
Section 161 deals with the offence of bribery involving public servants. It addresses the act of a public servant accepting or agreeing to accept a bribe for performing an official act or for showing favor to someone in discharge of their duty.
                                                                                           Summary:- This section makes it a punishable offence for a public servant to accept or agree to accept a bribe, or for anyone to offer or agree to offer a bribe to a public servant in exchange for a specific favor or action that pertains to their official duties.
legal_elements: Or offering bribe to public servant
example_scenarios: A government official accepts money from a contractor in exchange for awarding a construction project without proper bidding.
A citizen offers money to a police officer to avoid a traffic violation ticket.

title: Taking or Giving False Statement to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes making false statements or using false documents to mislead public servants during investigations.
section_text:
Section 162 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860 deals with the punishment for "taking a false statement" or "using a false document" to mislead or to deceive. This section is primarily concerned with preventing the misuse of the judicial or investigative process by individuals who may try to mislead authorities by providing false evidence or statements during investigations.

Summary:- The section makes it an offence to offer false evidence or give a false statement before a police officer, typically during an investigation. This can include any act of making or attempting to make a false statement or producing fabricated evidence. It is designed to maintain the integrity of the legal system by preventing the abuse of authority.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately gives false information to police about an alibi to protect a friend involved in a crime.
An individual submits forged documents to a government office to support a false claim for benefits.

title: Giving False Evidence in Judicial Proceedings

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes deliberately providing false evidence or lying during judicial proceedings.
section_text:
Section 163 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for giving false evidence in a case. It specifically addresses situations where a person deliberately provides misleading information or lies in a legal proceeding to create a false narrative.

Summary:- This section makes it an offense to knowingly and intentionally give false evidence during judicial proceedings. The provision is meant to prevent the administration of justice from being undermined by dishonest practices. If an individual is found guilty under this section, they are subject to a penalty as prescribed by the law.
example_scenarios: A witness testifies under oath with fabricated details about a crime scene to implicate an innocent person.
An expert witness deliberately provides false technical evidence in a civil case to favor one party.

title: Recording of Confessions and Statements by Magistrate

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Affecting The Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency And Morals.

description: Provides procedure for magistrates to record confessions and statements, ensuring voluntariness and legal compliance.
section_text:
This section deals with the recording of confessions and statements by a magistrate. It provides the procedure and conditions under which confessions or statements made by an accused or a witness can be recorded.

Summary:- Section 164 allows a Magistrate to record a confession made by an accused person, or a statement made by a witness, in the presence of the Magistrate. The section ensures that any confession made by an accused person is voluntary, and not coerced, tortured, or induced by any form of threat or promise. The statement made should be recorded in the manner prescribed under this section, and the person making the confession or statement must be informed of the consequences of such a confession or statement.
example_scenarios: A magistrate carefully records a suspect's confession after ensuring they understand their rights and the confession is voluntary.
Before a trial, a witness's statement is officially recorded by a magistrate to preserve evidence.

title: Public Servant Obtaining Valuable Thing Without Consideration (Repealed)

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences By Or Relating To Public Servants

description: Dealt with public servants obtaining valuable things without adequate consideration (now repealed and replaced by Prevention of Corruption Act).
section_text:
Section 165 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the criminal misconduct of public servants in relation to the acceptance of valuable things without adequate consideration. This section was aimed at preventing corruption among public servants. However, it has now been repealed and replaced by the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.

Summary:- Previously, IPC Section 165 stated that if a public servant obtained any valuable thing from another person in connection with their official duties without paying proper value or consideration, it would be considered an offence. This section was primarily introduced to curb corruption, bribery, and misuse of power by public officials.

However, since the introduction of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988, IPC Section 165 has been repealed, and similar provisions now fall under Section 11 of the Prevention of Corruption Act, 1988.
example_scenarios: A government official accepts expensive gifts from a contractor in exchange for favorable treatment in awarding contracts.
A public servant receives property transfers from a citizen seeking to influence an official decision.

title: Public Servant Knowingly Disobeying Law Causing Injury

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences By Or Relating To Public Servants

description: Penalizes public servants who knowingly disobey laws, causing injury or harm to individuals.
section_text:
Section 166 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the actions of public servants who knowingly disobey the law in a way that causes injury or harm to individuals. It penalizes government officials who, while discharging their duties, willfully break legal provisions, leading to injustice or oppression.

Summary:- This section ensures accountability among public servants by penalizing them for intentional violations of the law. It applies when a public official, despite knowing the legal provisions, disobeys them and causes harm to individuals or society.
example_scenarios: A police officer deliberately ignores court orders to release a detainee, causing illegal detention and harm.
A municipal official knowingly approves construction violating zoning laws, leading to safety hazards for residents.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Document

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences By Or Relating To Public Servants

description: Punishes public servants who knowingly prepare false documents or records with intent to cause harm.
section_text:
Section 167 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the actions of public servants who prepare false documents with the intent to cause harm or injury. It specifically targets public servants who frame incorrect records or documents knowingly to mislead or defraud.

Summary:- This section applies to cases where a public servant, whose duty is to prepare or maintain a record or document, knowingly falsifies it with dishonest intent. The law ensures accountability and prevents corruption by penalizing officials who abuse their position.
example_scenarios: A revenue officer deliberately creates false land records to transfer property illegally to a relative.
A police officer fabricates evidence documents to implicate an innocent person in a criminal case.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Engaging in Trade

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences By Or Relating To Public Servants

description: Prohibits public servants from engaging in trade or business contrary to their official duties.
section_text:
Section 168 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the unlawful engagement of public servants in trade. It prohibits a public servant from engaging in any trade, business, or commercial activity while holding a government position if it is contrary to their official duties.

Summary:- This section ensures that public servants do not engage in private commercial activities that could create conflicts of interest or affect their impartiality. It is meant to maintain the integrity of the public office and prevent misuse of power for personal financial gain.
example_scenarios: A government procurement officer runs a private company that bids for contracts from their own department.
A municipal commissioner operates a construction business that receives preferential treatment for city projects.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Purchasing Property

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences By Or Relating To Public Servants

description: Prohibits public servants from purchasing or bidding for property they are responsible for selling or disposing of.
section_text:
This section prohibits a public servant from purchasing or bidding for any property they are responsible for selling or disposing of in an official capacity. It ensures that public servants do not misuse their position for personal gain in matters related to state or public property.

Summary:- A public servant who is responsible for selling, auctioning, or disposing of any property must not attempt to purchase or bid for the same property, directly or indirectly. If they do, they are liable for punishment under this section.
example_scenarios: A government officer overseeing the auction of confiscated property secretly bids on the items through a relative.
A municipal official responsible for disposing of city-owned land arranges to purchase it at below-market value.

title: Personating a Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes falsely representing oneself as a public servant and exercising authority under false pretense.
section_text:
This section deals with cases where a person falsely represents themselves as a public servant and acts under the assumed authority of such a position.

Summary:- Any individual who fraudulently pretends to be a public servant and exercises authority under such a false pretense commits an offense under this section. This law ensures that individuals do not misuse the trust and authority associated with government officials to deceive others.
example_scenarios: A person dresses as a police officer and sets up a fake checkpoint to extort money from motorists.
An individual claims to be a tax inspector to gain entry into homes and steal valuables.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token of Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes wearing official uniform or carrying token of public servant with fraudulent intent.
section_text:
Section 171 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with wearing garb or carrying a token used by a public servant with fraudulent intent. It penalizes any individual who falsely represents themselves as a public servant by wearing an official uniform or carrying an official token with the intent to commit fraud or gain undue advantage.

Summary:- This section prohibits impersonation of public servants by wearing their uniform or using any official symbols or tokens. The law aims to prevent fraudulent activities that could mislead people or cause harm under the pretense of official authority.
example_scenarios: A person wears a police uniform to intimidate shopkeepers into giving them free goods.
An individual carries a forged government ID card to gain unauthorized access to a restricted area.

title: Absconding to Avoid Service of Summons

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes absconding or hiding to avoid being served with summons, notice, or legal order.
section_text:
Section 172 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with cases where a person deliberately avoids receiving a summons, notice, or any legal order issued by a public servant. The section is designed to ensure compliance with legal procedures by penalizing individuals who attempt to evade judicial or administrative actions.

Summary:- If a person knowingly absconds or hides to avoid being served with a summons, notice, or any legal order from a court or public servant, they can be punished under this section. The law aims to prevent obstruction of justice and ensure the proper functioning of legal and administrative proceedings.
example_scenarios: A person deliberately changes residence to avoid receiving a court summons for a traffic violation case.
An individual hides from process servers attempting to deliver a legal notice about a property dispute.

title: Obstructing Production of Document to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes obstructing police officer in producing documents or electronic records required for investigation.
section_text:
Section 173 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the obstruction of a police officer in the discharge of their lawful duties, specifically by preventing the production of a document or electronic record that is legally required.

Summary:- This section penalizes any person who prevents or obstructs the production of documents or electronic records that a public servant, particularly a police officer, requires during an investigation or inquiry. This obstruction hampers the legal process and is treated as an offence under IPC.
example_scenarios: A business owner refuses to provide financial records to police investigating a fraud case.
An individual deliberately destroys digital files that were subpoenaed for a criminal investigation.

title: Non-Reporting of Suspicious Death or Disappearance

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Affecting The Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency And Morals.

description: Mandates investigation and reporting of deaths or disappearances under suspicious or unnatural circumstances.
section_text:
This section deals with the offence of non-reporting of death or disappearance of a person in certain circumstances, particularly when the death or disappearance occurs under suspicious or unnatural conditions.

Summary:- Section 174 of the IPC mandates that a police officer must conduct an investigation and make a report if a person dies or is found to be missing under suspicious or unnatural circumstances, such as suicide, murder, or accident. Its a part of ensuring public safety and preventing foul play in cases of death or disappearance.
example_scenarios: Police must investigate a body found with unexplained injuries in a public park.
Authorities are required to file a report when a person disappears under circumstances suggesting foul play.

title: Refusing to Comply with Public Servant's Order

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes refusal to comply with lawful orders or directions given by a public servant.
section_text:
This section deals with failure to comply with a public servants order. It addresses situations where a person refuses to comply with the law or legal orders given by a public servant.

Summary:- A person who, without lawful excuse, refuses or neglects to comply with a public servants lawful order or direction is considered to have committed an offence under this section. It aims to ensure that public servants' lawful instructions are followed for the proper functioning of the legal and administrative system.
example_scenarios: A person refuses to follow a traffic police officer's lawful direction to stop at a checkpoint.
An individual neglects to comply with a municipal officer's order to remove an illegal construction.

title: Refusing to Provide Information or Produce Person/Document

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes refusal to provide information or produce person/document when legally required by public servant.
section_text:
Section 176 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for refusing to provide information or failing to produce a person or a document when required by law.

Summary:- This section punishes any individual who, when legally bound to provide a specific information or produce a person, fails to do so. This section applies when a person refuses to answer a lawful inquiry or withholds information that could be used to identify a person under investigation.
example_scenarios: A witness refuses to provide information to police about a crime they witnessed, despite legal obligation.
A company executive fails to produce financial documents subpoenaed by a government investigator.

title: Providing False Information to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes providing false information or refusing to provide correct information to public authorities.
section_text:
This section deals with the offense of providing false information or refusing to provide information to public authorities. Specifically, it punishes a person who refuses or neglects to provide a correct answer or gives false information when asked by a public servant.

Summary:- IPC Section 177 punishes individuals who either fail to answer a question asked by a public servant in the course of their duty or who deliberately provide false information to such a public servant.
example_scenarios: A person gives false personal details to a census officer during a population survey.
An applicant deliberately provides incorrect information on a government license application form.

title: Refusing to Answer Question by Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes refusal to answer questions put by public servant who has authority to ask them.
section_text:
Section 178 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of refusing to answer questions put by a public servant when they have the authority to ask them.

Summary:- This section punishes an individual who refuses to answer a question posed by a public servant who is legally authorized to ask that question. The refusal must be without lawful excuse.
example_scenarios: A suspect refuses to answer basic identifying questions during a police investigation without valid reason.
A witness in an official inquiry remains silent when asked relevant questions by an investigating officer.

title: Refusing to Answer When Legally Bound to Answer

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes refusal to answer questions when legally required to answer, obstructing legal proceedings.
section_text:
Refusing to answer questions, when legally bound to answer
                                                                             Summary:- Section 179 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the refusal of a person to answer any question that they are legally required to answer. It applies when a person is required to provide information, and their refusal to do so can obstruct legal proceedings or investigations. The refusal to answer is punishable under this section.
example_scenarios: A court witness refuses to answer questions during testimony despite being legally compelled to do so.
An expert witness appointed by the court remains silent when asked technical questions relevant to the case.

title: Refusing to Provide Information to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Criminalizes refusal to provide information required by public servant under law.
section_text:
The section deals with the offense of refusing to provide information to a public servant when required under law.

Summary:- Section 180 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of refusing to provide information that is required by a public servant under law. The individual must have been legally asked to provide the information, and refusal to do so could lead to prosecution.
example_scenarios: A landlord refuses to provide tenant information to municipal authorities conducting a housing survey.
A business owner declines to share employment records with labor department inspectors.

title: Giving False Information on Oath or Affirmation

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes knowingly giving false information under oath or affirmation in judicial or administrative proceedings.
section_text:
Section 181 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of giving false information on oath or affirmation. It specifically addresses a situation where a person gives false information in relation to a matter which is intended to be used in a judicial or administrative proceeding. This section makes it a criminal offense to knowingly make a false statement while under oath or affirmation.

Summary:- This section applies to situations where an individual, after being sworn in or affirming to tell the truth, deliberately provides false information, typically in a legal or official context. It serves as a safeguard against misleading judicial and administrative bodies.
example_scenarios: A witness in a court case deliberately provides false information about an alibi while under oath.
An individual makes a false declaration in an affidavit submitted to a government office.

title: False Information to Public Servant Causing Harm

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes providing false information to public servant with intent to cause harm or trigger investigation.
section_text:
Section 182 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for false information given to a public servant with the intention of causing harm, or leading to an investigation. It criminalizes the act of providing false reports or statements to a public officer with the intent to harm someone or mislead the authorities.

Summary:
This section specifically targets individuals who provide false information to public authorities (e.g., police, government officials) that could lead to actions such as wrongful investigations, accusations, or legal consequences. The goal is to prevent the misuse of legal procedures and ensure the integrity of the public service system.
example_scenarios: A person falsely reports a neighbor for drug dealing to police out of personal vendetta.
An individual provides fabricated information to a tax officer to initiate an audit against a business rival.

title: Obstructing Public Servant in Discharge of Duties

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes intentional obstruction of public servant while performing lawful duties.
section_text:
Section 183 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for obstructing a public servant in the discharge of his public duties. It aims to deter individuals from obstructing government officials or public servants while they are performing their lawful duties.

Summary:
This section states that if a person intentionally obstructs a public servant while performing their duty, they can be punished. The punishment may involve a fine or imprisonment, depending on the nature of the obstruction.
example_scenarios: A person physically blocks a health inspector from entering a restaurant for inspection.
An individual refuses to allow a municipal officer to measure land for official purposes.

title: Dangerous Driving of Vehicle

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Affecting The Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency And Morals.

description: Punishes driving a vehicle dangerously on public road endangering human life, safety, or property.
section_text:
This section deals with the act of driving a vehicle dangerously, either on a public road or in an area where such driving can cause harm to the public. It pertains to driving under circumstances that may endanger human life, safety of others, or property.

Summary:
Section 184 of the IPC prescribes punishment for driving a vehicle in a manner that causes danger to the public, endangering life, safety, or property. It applies to dangerous driving of all types of vehicles such as cars, motorcycles, and bicycles, among others.
example_scenarios: A driver speeds through a crowded market area, narrowly missing pedestrians.
A motorcyclist performs dangerous stunts on a busy highway during peak traffic.

title: Driving Under Influence of Alcohol or Drugs

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Affecting The Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency And Morals.

description: Punishes driving or being in charge of motor vehicle while under influence of alcohol or drugs.
section_text:
Driving, or being in charge of a motor vehicle, when under the influence of alcohol or drugs.

Summary: This section of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of driving or being in charge of a motor vehicle while under the influence of alcohol or any drug that impairs one's ability to drive safely. It specifically addresses situations where a person is found driving with a blood alcohol content above the prescribed limit or is under the influence of any narcotic substance.
example_scenarios: A person is caught driving a car after consuming alcohol at a party, failing a breathalyzer test.
A truck driver is found operating a commercial vehicle while under the influence of narcotics.

title: Obstructing Public Servant in Discharge of Functions

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions through force or threats.
section_text:
Obstructing public servant in discharge of public functions.

Summary: This section deals with the offence of obstructing a public servant in the discharge of their official duties. It seeks to penalize individuals who intentionally impede the work of a government official or public servant while they are performing their lawful duties.
example_scenarios: A crowd physically prevents a revenue officer from seizing property as part of a legal order.
An individual threatens a forest officer to stop them from investigating illegal logging.

title: Omission to Furnish Information to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes failing to provide information or providing false information when required by public servant.
section_text:
This section deals with the duty of individuals to provide truthful and timely information to a public servant. It criminalizes the act of either failing to provide such information or providing false details when required by a public servant in the performance of their duties.

Summary:
Section 187 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the responsibility of individuals to give truthful and accurate information when asked by a public servant. If someone knowingly omits to provide necessary information or deliberately provides false information to a public servant, it constitutes an offence under this section.
example_scenarios: A landlord fails to provide tenant details to municipal authorities conducting a housing survey.
A business owner gives false employment records to labor department inspectors.
vector_search_terms: failure to disclose to public servant

title: Disobedience to Order Duly Promulgated by Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Punishes disobedience to lawful orders issued by public servants that cause harm to public safety, health, or order.
section_text:
Section 188 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the disobedience to an order duly promulgated by a public servant.

Summary:
This section addresses the act of disobeying or violating an order issued by a public servant that is legally binding. If someone knowingly disregards or refuses to comply with an order passed by a public servant, it may lead to punishment under this section.
example_scenarios: A person refuses to comply with a curfew order imposed by the district magistrate during a riot situation.
An individual disobeys a health department order to quarantine during a pandemic outbreak.

title: Threat of Injury to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts Of The Lawful Authority Of Public Servants

description: Penalizes threatening to harm a public servant or their family in retaliation for performing official duties.
section_text:
Threat of injury to a public servant.
Summary: This section penalizes anyone who threatens to harm a public servant or their family in retaliation for the public servant performing their duty. The threat can be either physical harm, injury, or harm to reputation.
example_scenarios: A person threatens a tax officer with physical harm after their business is penalized for tax evasion.
An individual sends threatening messages to a police officer's family after the officer arrests them.

title: Magistrate's Power to Take Cognizance of Offence
description: Empowers magistrate to take cognizance of offences based on police report, complaint, or own knowledge.
section_text:
Section 190 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with how a Magistrate may take cognizance (i.e., officially recognize) of an offence. This section specifies that a Magistrate can take cognizance of an offence based on a complaint, a police report, or through his own knowledge.

Summary
This section empowers a Magistrate to take cognizance of an offence:

Upon a police report: If the police report indicates that an offence has been committed.
Upon a complaint: If a person lodges a complaint alleging the commission of an offence.
Upon his own knowledge or suspicion: If the Magistrate is aware or has knowledge of the offence being committed.
In essence, it grants the Magistrate authority to begin judicial proceedings in cases of crime.
example_scenarios: A magistrate takes cognizance of a theft case based on a police report.
A magistrate initiates proceedings based on a citizen's complaint about assault.

title: Giving False Evidence in Judicial Proceedings

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes intentionally giving false evidence or fabricating evidence in court or judicial proceedings.
section_text:
This section deals with the act of giving false evidence during judicial proceedings. It is a criminal offense to intentionally provide false evidence or statements that are likely to mislead the court, tribunal, or any other judicial body.

Summary:
Section 191 of the Indian Penal Code punishes any person who, in a court or before a person authorized by law, gives false evidence intentionally or knowingly. This includes the act of fabricating evidence, lying under oath, or providing false information, all of which can affect the fairness of judicial proceedings.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately gives false testimony about an alibi in a murder trial.
An expert witness provides fabricated technical evidence in a civil case.

title: Fabricating False Evidence

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes fabricating false evidence or making false statements to cause wrongful loss or harm in legal proceedings.
section_text:
This section deals with the punishment for fabricating false evidence or making false statements or declarations with the intent of causing wrongful loss or harm to others in legal proceedings.

Summary:
Section 192 of the IPC addresses the act of knowingly or willfully making a false statement or fabricating false evidence in relation to any legal proceedings. The intention behind such an act is to deceive the judicial authorities to either cause harm or gain an unfair advantage. It emphasizes the importance of honesty and truth in legal processes.
example_scenarios: A person creates fake documents to support a false property claim in court.
An individual manufactures false witness statements to implicate someone in a criminal case.

title: Punishment for False Evidence

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes giving false evidence in judicial proceedings, including fabricating evidence to mislead legal process.
section_text:
Section 193 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the crime of fabricating false evidence, which involves intentionally creating or altering evidence with the aim of misleading the legal process. This section aims to prevent the tampering or falsification of evidence, which is a serious crime as it undermines the justice system.

Summary:
This section makes it an offense for any person to fabricate or falsify evidence, such as documents or testimonies, with the intent to mislead a court or tribunal. The act of fabricating false evidence could be aimed at any stage of legal proceedings, whether civil or criminal, and is seen as a serious attack on the integrity of the legal system.
example_scenarios: A person alters financial records submitted as evidence in a fraud case.
An individual creates fake audio recordings to support a false allegation in court.

title: Giving False Evidence in Judicial Proceeding

chapter.chapter_name: Of False Evidence And Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishes giving false evidence in any court of law, tribunal, or lawful proceeding.
section_text:
Section 194 of the Indian Penal Code deals with punishment for false evidence. It criminalizes giving false evidence in judicial proceedings, such as in courts or during investigations.

Summary:
This section punishes a person who gives false evidence in any court of law, tribunal, or any lawful proceeding. False evidence includes misleading statements, lies, or fabricated documents intended to deceive the court.
example_scenarios: A witness provides fabricated details about a crime scene during trial testimony.
An individual submits forged documents as evidence in a civil lawsuit.

title: False Evidence for Capital Offence Conviction

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for intentionally giving or fabricating false evidence to secure conviction for a capital offence punishable by death.
section_text: This section deals with the punishment for a person who intentionally gives or fabricates false evidence in a case with the intention of securing the conviction of someone for a capital offence (a crime that is punishable by death). The section seeks to prevent false testimonies or fabricated evidence that may lead to the wrongful conviction of an innocent person.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately provides false testimony about seeing someone commit a murder, knowing the accused is innocent, to ensure they receive the death penalty.
A person plants fabricated evidence at a crime scene to frame another individual for a capital offence punishable by death.

title: Using Forged Documents in Legal Proceedings

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Offence of using forged documents or inducing false evidence in legal proceedings or against public authorities.
section_text: Section 196 deals with offences related to contempt of lawful authority of public servants, as well as offences involving documents that are produced or given in evidence during proceedings.
example_scenarios: A person submits a forged property document as evidence in a court case to support their false claim.
An individual creates false witness statements to obstruct a police investigation into a criminal matter.

title: False Evidence in Judicial Proceedings

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Criminalizes giving false evidence or fabricating evidence to mislead authorities in judicial proceedings.
section_text: IPC Section 197 criminalizes the act of knowingly giving false evidence in a judicial proceeding or fabricating evidence with the intention of misleading the authorities or causing wrongful legal consequences.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately gives false testimony under oath during a trial to protect the actual culprit.
A party to a lawsuit creates fake documents to support their false claims in court.

title: Prosecution Protection for Public Servants

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Requires government sanction before prosecuting public servants for actions in official duties.
section_text: Section 198 of the Indian Penal Code provides for protection to judges and public servants from being prosecuted for actions done in the discharge of their official duties. The section requires previous sanction from the government before a public servant or a judge can be prosecuted for actions they take in the course of their official duties.
example_scenarios: A citizen attempts to file a criminal case against a police officer for actions taken during an arrest without obtaining required government sanction.
A case against a judge for a decision made in court is dismissed because proper sanction was not obtained before prosecution.

title: False Statement in Legal Declaration

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for false statements made in declarations receivable as evidence under law.
section_text: False statement made in declaration which is by law receivable as evidence.
example_scenarios: A person makes a false statement in a sworn affidavit submitted to court regarding their financial status.
An individual provides false information in a statutory declaration required for a government application.

title: False Statement Under Oath

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for making false statements under oath or affirmation in judicial proceedings.
section_text: Using a false statement or declaration under oath or affirmation in a judicial proceeding.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately lies while testifying under oath during a trial.
A defendant provides false information in a sworn deposition during discovery proceedings.

title: Defamation of Public Servants
description: Punishment for defamation against public servants and their families.
section_text: Section 201 deals with the punishment for defamation if the words are published or spoken by any person concerning a public servant, his spouse, or any member of his family.
example_scenarios: A person spreads false rumors about a government officer taking bribes, damaging their reputation.
Someone publishes a malicious article about a judge's family members with intent to harm their standing.

title: False Statement to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for causing public servant to believe false information regarding official duties.
section_text: Section 202 pertains to the punishment for Barnett's False Statement, which involves causing a public servant to believe in a lie or fraud concerning his official duty.
example_scenarios: A person provides false information to a police officer to mislead an investigation.
An individual makes a fraudulent complaint to a government agency to cause unnecessary action.

title: Perjury in Court

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for intentional false testimony under oath in court or before magistrate.
section_text: Section 203 relates to the punishment for perjury by a witness in a court of justice or before a Magistrate, if such perjury is committed intentionally with the intent to deceive.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately lies under oath during a trial to protect a friend.
An expert witness provides false testimony about evidence to influence the outcome of a case.

title: False Entry by Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for defrauding through false entries in registers by deceiving public servants.
section_text: Section 204 deals with the punishment for acts intended to defraud a person in respect of property or inheritance, by causing a public servant to commit a false entry in any register or record.
example_scenarios: A person convinces a government clerk to make false entries in land records to transfer property illegally.
An individual deceives a public official into recording false information in official registers to claim inheritance rights.

title: Obstruction of Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants

description: Punishment for using force or threats to obstruct public servants in official duties.
section_text: Section 205 pertains to the punishment for obstruction of a public servant in the discharge of his duty, which includes using force or threats to prevent a public servant from carrying out their official duties
example_scenarios: A person physically blocks a police officer from making an arrest.
An individual threatens a government official to prevent them from conducting an inspection.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust
description: Punishment for fraudulent conversion of entrusted property for personal benefit.
section_text: Section 206 deals with the punishment for criminal breach of trust, where a person fraudulently converts property entrusted to them and uses it for their own benefit or for another person.
example_scenarios: A bank manager uses customer deposits for personal investments without authorization.
A property caretaker sells entrusted assets and keeps the proceeds for themselves.

title: Criminal Abstraction of Property
description: Punishment for fraudulently taking another's property without consent.
section_text: Section 207 pertains to the punishment for criminal abstraction of property, where a person fraudulently takes away property belonging to another without their consent.
example_scenarios: An employee takes company equipment under false pretenses of official use but keeps it for personal use.
A person uses deception to obtain someone's property by pretending to be the rightful owner.

title: Neglect of Official Duty

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants

description: Punishment for public servants intentionally neglecting official duties causing loss.
section_text: Section 208 deals with the punishment for a public servant who neglects to perform their official duty intentionally, leading to loss or mismanagement of public funds or property.
example_scenarios: A government accountant intentionally fails to process payments, causing financial loss to the department.
A public official deliberately ignores safety inspections, resulting in public property damage.

title: Influencing Public Servant Unlawfully

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants

description: Punishment for causing public servant to act against legal obligations causing harm.
section_text: Section 209 relates to the punishment for causing a public servant to act in a manner that is against their legal obligations, thereby causing harm or injury to any person.
example_scenarios: A person bribes a government official to ignore regulatory violations that result in public harm.
An individual threatens a public servant to make decisions contrary to their legal duties, causing injury to others.

title: Using Forged Documents

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using forged or fraudulently obtained documents required by law to be genuine.
section_text: Section 210 pertains to the punishment for using a forged document or fraudulently obtaining a document that is required by law to be genuine.
example_scenarios: A person uses a forged educational certificate to obtain a government job.
An individual presents a fraudulently obtained license to operate a business illegally.

title: Using Forged Coin or Banknote

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using counterfeit currency with intent to defraud.
section_text: Section 211 deals with the punishment for using a forged coin or banknote with intent to defraud.
example_scenarios: A person knowingly uses fake ₹500 notes to purchase goods at a market.
An individual attempts to deposit counterfeit coins in a bank account.

title: Possessing Illegally Obtained Property
description: Punishment for possessing, using, concealing, or transferring stolen or fraudulent property.
section_text: Section 211 deals with the punishment for using a forged coin or banknote with intent to defraud
example_scenarios: A person knowingly buys a stolen car and registers it under their name.
An individual stores stolen electronic goods in their warehouse for resale.

title: Obstructing Public Works or Railways

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals

description: Punishment for placing obstructions on roads, tracks, or structures to hinder public infrastructure.
section_text: Section 213 deals with the punishment for obstructing a railway or other public work by placing obstructions on roads, tracks, or structures.
example_scenarios: A person places large rocks on railway tracks to disrupt train services.
Protesters block a major highway with vehicles to obstruct traffic.

title: Hindering Police Officers

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants

description: Punishment for refusing to assist or providing false information to police officers during duty.
section_text: Section 214 pertains to the punishment for hindering a police officer in the discharge of their duty, such as refusing to assist or providing false information.
example_scenarios: A witness deliberately gives false information to police investigating a crime.
A person refuses to provide identification when lawfully asked by a police officer.

title: Hoarding Essential Goods

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals

description: Punishment for artificially creating scarcity of essential goods to increase prices.
section_text: Section 215 deals with the punishment for hoarding or artificially creating a scarcity of essential goods, services, or resources, thereby causing a sudden increase in their price or value.
example_scenarios: A trader buys large quantities of medicines during a pandemic to sell at inflated prices.
A business stockpiles fuel to create artificial shortage and increase market prices.

title: Impersonating Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants

description: Punishment for assuming identity or uniform of public servant with intent to commit fraud.
section_text: Section 216 pertains to the punishment for impersonating a public servant, such as assuming their identity or uniform with intent to commit fraud.
example_scenarios: A person wears a police uniform to extort money from shopkeepers.
An individual poses as a government official to collect fake fines from citizens.

title: Tampering with Evidence

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for fabricating or tampering with evidence to mislead judicial inquiry.
section_text: Section 217 deals with the punishment for fabricating or tampering with evidence, with the intent to mislead a judicial inquiry or investigation.
example_scenarios: A person alters documents submitted as evidence in a court case.
An individual destroys physical evidence to protect someone from criminal charges.

title: False Records by Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for public servant creating false records to shield someone from punishment or prevent property forfeiture.
section_text: This section deals with situations where a public servant knowingly creates false records or documents with the intention of saving someone from legal punishment or preventing property forfeiture.
example_scenarios: A police officer falsifies arrest records to help a suspect avoid charges.
A revenue officer creates false documents to prevent seizure of property in a legal case.

title: False Report by Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for public servant making false report or decision to cause harm.
section_text: Section 219 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) states that if a public servant, who has the duty to make a report or decision regarding any person under the law, deliberately makes a false report or decision with the intent to cause harm or injury, they shall be punished.
legal_elements: duty to make report or decision
example_scenarios: A government officer submits a false inspection report to get an innocent business shut down.
A judge deliberately gives a wrong judgment to harm a party in a case.

title: Wrongful Confinement by Public Servant
description: Punishment for public servant wrongfully confining person without lawful grounds.
section_text: Section 220 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) states that if a person, who is legally empowered as a public servant to confine or imprison individuals, corruptly or maliciously commits an act of wrongful confinement knowing that there is no lawful ground for the same, they shall be punished under this section.
example_scenarios: A police officer detains a person without any charges or legal basis due to personal vendetta.
A jail official keeps a prisoner beyond their release date intentionally.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for public servant intentionally failing to apprehend or confine accused person.
section_text: Section 221 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the intentional omission by a public servant to apprehend or keep in confinement a person charged with an offense. It punishes a public servant who knowingly allows an accused person to escape justice.

punishment.imprisonment: up to 7 years (if capital offence), up to 3 years (if life/10 years), up to 2 years (other cases)

example_scenarios: A police officer deliberately fails to arrest a known criminal who is charged with murder.
A jail guard intentionally leaves a cell door unlocked allowing a prisoner to escape.

title: Intentional Omission Leading to Escape

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for public servant intentionally allowing charged or convicted person to escape.
section_text: Section 222 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the intentional omission by a public servant to apprehend or keep in confinement a person charged with or convicted of an offence, with the intent to facilitate their escape.

punishment.imprisonment: up to 10 years (if death penalty), up to 3 years (if life/10 years), up to 2 years (other cases)

example_scenarios: A prison officer deliberately fails to lock a cell, allowing a convicted murderer to escape.
A police officer intentionally delays arresting a fugitive, giving them time to flee.

title: Escape Due to Negligence

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for public servant negligence allowing prisoner escape from lawful custody.
section_text: Section 223 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the escape or rescue of a prisoner due to the negligence of a public servant who is responsible for their custody.

punishment.imprisonment: up to 7 years (if capital offence), up to 2 years (other cases)

example_scenarios: A prison guard falls asleep on duty, allowing a prisoner to escape.
A police officer leaves handcuffs unlocked, enabling a detainee to flee.

title: Escaping Lawful Custody

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for escaping from lawful custody or resisting arrest.
section_text: Section 224 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the intentional act of escaping from lawful custody or resisting arrest by a person who is legally bound to be in confinement.
example_scenarios: A prisoner breaks out of jail by climbing over the wall.
A suspect being arrested physically struggles with police officers to avoid being handcuffed.

title: Obstructing Lawful Apprehension

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for obstructing arrest or helping someone escape from lawful custody.
section_text: Section 225 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, deals with resistance or obstruction to lawful apprehension of any person, or escape of a person from lawful custody.

punishment.imprisonment: up to 10 years (if death penalty), up to 7 years (if life imprisonment), up to 3 years (other cases)

example_scenarios: A person physically blocks police from arresting a family member.
An individual provides a getaway car to help a prisoner escape from custody.

title: Wrongful Return of Warrant

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for returning warrant without execution without lawful excuse.
section_text: Section 226 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) states that if a person who is legally bound to execute a warrant of any authority returns the warrant without executing it, without a lawful excuse, they shall be punished under this section.
example_scenarios: A police officer receives an arrest warrant but returns it without attempting to locate the suspect.
A court official fails to execute a search warrant and returns it without valid reason.

title: Discharge of Accused (CrPC)
description: CrPC provision for Sessions Court to discharge accused if insufficient grounds for trial.
section_text: Section 227 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) does not exist in the IPC itself but is a provision under the Code of Criminal Procedure (CrPC), 1973. It deals with the power of the Sessions Court to discharge an accused if there is insufficient ground for proceeding with the trial.

title: Insulting Judicial Proceeding

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for intentionally insulting or interrupting public servant in judicial proceeding.
section_text: Whoever intentionally insults or interrupts any public servant while they are sitting in a judicial proceeding shall be punished.
example_scenarios: A spectator shouts abusive language at a judge during a court hearing.
A lawyer repeatedly interrupts the judge despite warnings to stop.

title: Failure to Appear as Witness

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for intentionally failing to appear in court to testify when legally bound.
section_text: Section 229 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with a person who intentionally fails to appear in court to testify as required after having been legally bound to do so.
example_scenarios: A subpoenaed witness deliberately skips court date to avoid testifying.
An expert witness fails to appear for a scheduled court hearing without valid reason.

title: False Information to Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for providing false information to public servant to cause harm to another.
section_text: Section 230 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of providing false information to a public servant, with the intent to cause harm to another person by influencing the public servant's lawful use of power.
example_scenarios: A person falsely reports to police that their neighbor is involved in drug trafficking to get them arrested.
An individual provides fabricated information to a government officer to cause cancellation of someone's license.

title: Counterfeiting Coins

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for making or manufacturing counterfeit coins.
section_text: Section 231 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the offence of counterfeiting coins. It criminalizes the act of making or manufacturing counterfeit coins with the intention to use them as genuine currency or facilitate their circulation.
example_scenarios: A person operates a secret workshop to produce fake ₹10 coins.
A criminal gang manufactures counterfeit coins using specialized equipment.

title: Counterfeiting Indian Coins

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for counterfeiting coins that resemble legally recognized Indian currency.
section_text: Section 232 of the Indian Penal Code, 1860, deals with the punishment for counterfeiting Indian coins. It criminalizes the act of making or manufacturing counterfeit coins that resemble legally recognized Indian currency.
example_scenarios: A criminal produces fake ₹5 coins that look identical to genuine ones.
An individual operates a mint to create counterfeit Indian coins for circulation.

title: Possessing Counterfeiting Instruments

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for making, buying, selling, or possessing instruments to counterfeit Indian coin.
section_text: According to Section 233 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC), anyone who makes, buys, sells, or possesses any instrument with the intent to counterfeit Indian coin is committing an offense.
example_scenarios: A person is found with coin stamping machines used to produce fake coins.
An individual purchases specialized dyes for creating counterfeit currency.

title: Counterfeiting Process Involvement

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for counterfeiting Indian coins or assisting in counterfeiting process.
section_text: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, Indian coin shall be punished.
example_scenarios: A person operates the metal casting machine for a counterfeit coin operation.
An individual supplies raw materials knowing they will be used for making fake coins.

title: Possessing Counterfeiting Materials

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for possessing instruments or materials for counterfeiting Indian coin.
section_text: Section 235 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the possession of instruments or materials for counterfeiting Indian coin. It criminalizes the act of possessing any tool, instrument, or material with the intention of using it to counterfeit Indian currency.
example_scenarios: Police find specialized metal alloys and molds for making fake coins in a suspect's home.
A person stores chemical solutions used for coin plating in a counterfeit operation.

title: Possessing Counterfeit Coins

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for possessing counterfeit coins resembling Indian currency with intent to use.
section_text: Section 236 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with the situation where a person is in possession of counterfeit coins that resemble Indian currency, with the intention of using them as genuine.
example_scenarios: A person is caught with a bag of fake ₹10 coins intended for use in vending machines.
A shopkeeper knowingly keeps counterfeit coins to give as change to customers.

title: False Representation for Gain
description: Punishment for making false statement or representation to obtain money or property.
section_text: Section 237 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of making a false statement or false representation to obtain money or property. It criminalizes the act of intentionally deceiving someone with the intention of obtaining something of value.
example_scenarios: A person falsely claims to be a charity collector to obtain donations.
An individual misrepresents the condition of a car to sell it at a higher price.

title: Procedural Failure for Trial
description: Addresses procedural lapse when accused not properly committed for trial.
section_text: Section 238 of the Indian Penal Code deals with "Committed for trial". It outlines a situation where a person who is charged with an offense that may be tried by a Magistrate but is not brought before the Magistrate for trial, or if the person is not committed for trial by a Magistrate.

title: False Accusation of Crime

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for falsely accusing someone of a crime without legal grounds.
section_text: Section 239 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of falsely accusing someone of a crime when they have no legal grounds to do so.
example_scenarios: A person files a police report claiming their neighbor stole property, knowing it's false.
An individual accuses someone of assault to settle a personal grudge without evidence.

title: False Property Marking

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using false property marking to mislead about ownership or authenticity.
section_text: This section deals with the offense of using a false property marking to mislead others regarding the ownership or authenticity of a property.
example_scenarios: A seller puts fake brand labels on unbranded goods to sell at higher prices.
A person alters property documents with false stamps to claim ownership.

title: False Accusation with Malicious Intent

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for making false accusation of offence with intent to cause harm.
section_text: This section addresses the act of making a false charge or accusation against someone, specifically when the person making the false charge intends to cause harm to the reputation or well-being of the accused.
example_scenarios: A person files a false police report accusing their neighbor of theft to damage their reputation.
An individual makes false allegations against a business competitor to harm their professional standing.
vector_search_terms: wrongful allegation with intent to harm

title: Wrongful Confinement with Dishonest Intent
description: Punishment for wrongfully confining person with intent to dishonestly remove or retain.
section_text: Section 242 of the Indian Penal Code (IPC) deals with "dishonestly removing or keeping a person in confinement" without legal authority. This section addresses the act of detaining a person in a manner that is unlawful or in violation of their rights.
example_scenarios: A person locks someone in a room to force them to sign a property document.
Security guards detain a person without cause to extort money from them.

title: Disobedience of Court Order for Property Delivery

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for willfully disobeying court order to deliver property.
section_text: This section addresses the offence related to the disobedience of a lawful order of the court when a person fails to comply with the terms of an order issued by a court. It mainly pertains to situations where a person, having been directed by the court to deliver a specific document or item, refuses to do so.
example_scenarios: A person refuses to return property to its rightful owner as ordered by the court.
An individual fails to deliver documents required for a legal case despite court orders.

title: Fraudulent Use of False Instruments

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using false instruments to obtain money or property by deception.
section_text: Section 244 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of fraudulent use of false instruments for obtaining money or property by false means.
example_scenarios: A person uses forged documents to obtain a loan from a bank.
An individual presents fake certificates to secure a government job.

title: Discharge of Accused for Insufficient Evidence
description: Procedural provision for magistrate to discharge accused when insufficient evidence.
section_text: Section 245 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the procedure when the Magistrate finds that there is insufficient evidence to frame a charge in a case. It grants the Magistrate the discretion to discharge the accused.

title: Trial Procedure for Accused
description: Procedural law for informing accused of charges and conducting trial.
section_text: This section pertains to the procedure when a Magistrate orders an accused to appear in court for a trial in a case where the accused is being charged with an offence punishable with imprisonment or fine.

title: Obstructing Public Servant

chapter.chapter_name: Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants

description: Punishment for annoying or obstructing public servant in duty execution.
section_text: This section deals with the offence of public nuisance, specifically regarding the disruption of the process of a public servant in the performance of their duty. If someone obstructs or annoys a public servant who is executing a legal duty, this section applies.
example_scenarios: A person repeatedly interrupts a police officer during an investigation.
An individual creates disturbance during a court proceeding.

title: Acquittal Procedure
description: Procedural law for magistrate to acquit accused when not guilty.
section_text: Section 248 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the procedure when a magistrate decides not to convict the accused in a criminal trial. It outlines the actions a magistrate must take when an accused person is acquitted, especially in cases where the magistrate feels that there is insufficient evidence to convict.

title: Discharge for Absent Accused
description: Procedural provision for discharging accused absent from summary trial.
section_text: Section 249 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the provision for discharging a person when the accused is absent at the time of trial, or when the person who has been charged with an offence is not present before the court.

title: False Claims or Suits

chapter.chapter_name: False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice

description: Punishment for filing false claims or suits with malicious intent.
section_text: Section 250 of the Indian Penal Code pertains to the punishment for false claims or false suits. It deals with the situation when someone falsely initiates a lawsuit or makes a claim, and is held liable for the same.
example_scenarios: A person files a false property lawsuit to harass a neighbor.
An individual initiates a baseless legal claim to extort money from a company.

title: False Weights or Measures

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Weights and Measures

description: Punishment for making or publishing false weights or measures to defraud.
section_text: Section 251 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of making or publishing false weight or measure. This provision is intended to prevent fraudulent practices involving incorrect weights or measures that may deceive or harm consumers.
example_scenarios: A shopkeeper uses a weighing scale that shows 900 grams as 1 kilogram.
A manufacturer produces measuring tapes with incorrect markings.

title: Possessing False Weights or Measures

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Weights and Measures

description: Punishment for possessing false weights or measures with fraudulent intent.
section_text: This section deals with the possession of any false weight or measure with the intent to use it fraudulently.
example_scenarios: A shopkeeper keeps underweight weights to use during sales.
A trader stores measuring scales with altered calibrations.

title: Using False Weights or Measures

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Weights and Measures

description: Punishment for fraudulent use of false weights or measures in trade.
section_text: This section penalizes the fraudulent use of false weights or measures in trade or transactions.
example_scenarios: A vegetable vendor uses a manipulated scale to charge more for produce.
A fuel station uses altered pumps to dispense less fuel than shown.

title: Delivering Goods with False Weights

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Weights and Measures

description: Punishment for delivering goods using false weights or measures.
section_text: This section criminalizes the delivery of goods knowingly using a false weight or measure.
example_scenarios: A wholesaler delivers less quantity than ordered using false weights.
