title: Definition of Robbery
description: Elaborates on the crime of robbery, especially when force or threat is used to commit theft.
section_text: Section 390 of the Indian Penal Code elaborates on the crime of robbery, especially when force or threat of force is used to commit theft.
legal_elements: Theft becomes robbery if, in order to commit theft, or while committing theft, or while carrying away property obtained by theft, the offender voluntarily causes or attempts to cause: death/hurt/restraint OR fear of instant death/hurt/restraint.

Extortion becomes robbery if the offender is in the presence of the person put in fear, and commits the extortion by putting that person in fear of instant hurt, and by so doing induces them to deliver up the property.
example_scenarios: A thief snatches a woman's purse and pushes her to the ground, causing injury.
A person threatens a shopkeeper with a knife, demanding cash from the register immediately.

court_judgment_links.summary: Placeholder for a relevant Supreme Court judgment.

title: Dacoity
description: Robbery committed by a group of five or more people.
section_text: Section 391 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the act of dacoity, which involves committing robbery with a group of five or more people.
legal_elements: Group of five or more persons

punishment.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

example_scenarios: Five armed individuals enter a jewelry store, threaten the owner with weapons, and steal gold ornaments and cash worth several lakhs.
A group of seven people stop a cargo truck on a highway, assault the driver, and steal the entire shipment of electronic goods.

title: Robbery Causing Bodily Harm
description: Robbery using force or threat that causes bodily harm to the victim.
section_text: Section 392 of the Indian Penal Code focuses on robbery where the robber uses force or threatens harm to steal property, and when the act results in bodily harm to the victim.
example_scenarios: A robber pushes a woman to the ground during a purse snatching, causing her to fracture her wrist.
During a bank robbery, the assailant strikes a security guard with a blunt object while escaping with stolen cash.

title: Attempt to Commit Robbery
description: Attempting to commit robbery using force or threats.
section_text: Section 393 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of attempting robbery, involving either the use of force or threats to rob a person of their property.
example_scenarios: A man approaches a pedestrian with a knife demanding money, but the victim screams and bystanders intervene before any property is taken.
Two individuals try to break into a parked car to steal contents but are apprehended by police before gaining entry.

title: Robbery Causing Grievous Hurt
description: Robbery that causes grievous bodily harm to the victim.
section_text: Section 394 of the Indian Penal Code provides for punishment when a person commits robbery and causes grievous bodily harm to the victim in the process of committing the robbery.
example_scenarios: During a robbery at a convenience store, the assailant stabs the shopkeeper, causing life-threatening injuries while stealing cash from the register.
A robber attacks a elderly person during a snatching, resulting in multiple fractures and permanent disability.

title: Dacoity
description: Robbery committed by five or more persons with intent to steal.
section_text: Section 395 of the Indian Penal Code defines and punishes dacoity involving five or more individuals with the intention of committing robbery.
legal_elements: Group of five or more persons

punishment.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

example_scenarios: Six armed men raid a village household, tie up the family members, and loot their savings and jewelry.
A group of eight individuals hijack a delivery truck, assault the driver, and steal the entire cargo of mobile phones.

title: Dacoity with Murder
description: Dacoity during which murder is committed.
section_text: Section 396 of the Indian Penal Code deals with punishment for dacoity with murder. If a person commits dacoity and in the process murders someone, the act is punishable under this section.
legal_elements: Group of five or more persons

punishment.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

example_scenarios: During a bank robbery by six armed men, one of the robbers shoots and kills a security guard who tried to resist.
A group of dacoits attacking a wealthy household murder the homeowner when he attempts to defend his family and property.

title: Robbery with Deadly Weapon
description: Robbery committed using a deadly weapon.
section_text: Section 397 of the Indian Penal Code defines and punishes the use of deadly weapons while committing robbery. If a person commits robbery and uses a deadly weapon, such as a firearm, knife, or any object capable of causing death, they are liable for punishment under this section.
example_scenarios: A robber points a loaded pistol at a shopkeeper while demanding money from the cash register.
During a street mugging, the assailant threatens the victim with a knife while taking their wallet and phone.

title: Attempted Robbery with Deadly Weapon
description: Attempting robbery while armed with a deadly weapon.
section_text: Section 398 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for attempting robbery with a deadly weapon. The law applies when an individual attempts to commit robbery while armed with a deadly weapon.
example_scenarios: A man attempts to rob a bank while brandishing a gun but is apprehended by security before taking any money.
An individual tries to mug a pedestrian with a knife but the victim escapes and alerts the police.

title: Preparation for Dacoity
description: Making preparations to commit dacoity.
section_text: Section 399 of the Indian Penal Code defines and punishes preparing for robbery. The law criminalizes the act of making preparations to commit robbery.
example_scenarios: A group of six people are caught with weapons, maps of a bank, and detailed plans for a robbery they were preparing to execute.
Police raid a hideout and find firearms, masks, and written plans for attacking a jewelry store that was scheduled for the following night.

title: Belonging to Gang of Robbers
description: Membership in a gang that regularly commits robberies.
section_text: Section 400 of the Indian Penal Code defines and punishes membership in a gang of robbers. This section targets individuals who are part of a group that regularly commits robberies or engages in criminal activities.

punishment.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

example_scenarios: An individual is identified as a member of a notorious robbery gang that has committed multiple bank heists across the state.
A person is found to be regularly meeting with known dacoits and participating in planning sessions for criminal activities.

title: Belonging to Gang of Thieves
description: Membership in a gang involved in theft-related offenses.
section_text: Section 401 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for belonging to a gang of thieves. This section applies to individuals who are part of a group involved in stealing or committing theft-related offenses.

punishment.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 7 years

example_scenarios: A person is identified as a member of a organized shoplifting ring that targets retail stores across multiple cities.
An individual regularly participates in meetings and activities of a group known for coordinated pickpocketing and theft operations.

title: Assembling for Dacoity
description: Assembling or preparing to commit theft, burglary, or robbery.
section_text: Section 402 of the Indian Penal Code punishes those who prepare for committing theft, burglary, or robbery in a manner likely to cause harm or public disorder. It criminalizes the act of organizing or preparing for such crimes.
legal_elements: Preparation for theft, burglary or robbery
example_scenarios: A group of individuals is found with burglary tools, blueprints of a house, and communication devices while preparing to commit a robbery.
Police intercept a meeting where several people are discussing detailed plans to break into a warehouse and steal goods.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property
description: Dishonestly taking possession of property to permanently deprive the owner.
section_text: Section 403 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes the act of dishonest misappropriation of property. It applies when someone, without consent, takes possession of property with the intention to permanently deprive the rightful owner of it.
example_scenarios: A person finds a lost wallet and keeps the money instead of attempting to return it to the owner.
An employee uses company equipment for personal business without permission and without intention to return it.

title: Misappropriation of Deceased Person's Property
description: Dishonest misappropriation of a deceased person's property to deprive legal heirs.
section_text: Section 404 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the dishonest misappropriation or conversion of a deceased person's property, intending to deprive the legal heirs of their right to inherit.
example_scenarios: A caretaker takes possession of a deceased elderly person's jewelry and bank assets instead of transferring them to the legal heirs.
After a person's death, their business partner dishonestly converts joint business assets for personal use without informing the deceased's family.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust
description: Dishonest misappropriation of property entrusted to an individual.
section_text: Section 405 of the Indian Penal Code deals with criminal breach of trust. It criminalizes the act of dishonestly misappropriating or converting property entrusted to an individual by another person.
example_scenarios: A lawyer misuses client funds that were entrusted to them for a real estate transaction.
A financial advisor invests a client's money in unauthorized high-risk ventures for personal gain.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust
description: Dishonest misappropriation of entrusted property.
section_text: Section 406 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of criminal breach of trust, specifically when someone is entrusted with property or authority and misappropriates or dishonestly converts it.
example_scenarios: A property manager collects rent from tenants but fails to remit the funds to the property owner.
A family member entrusted with managing an elderly relative's finances uses the money for personal expenses without authorization.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Carrier
description: Breach of trust by carrier, wharfinger, or warehouse-keeper.
section_text: Section 407 of the Indian Penal Code deals with criminal breach of trust by a carrier, wharfinger, or warehouse-keeper. It criminalizes the dishonest misappropriation or conversion of goods entrusted to a carrier or warehouse keeper.
example_scenarios: A trucking company employee sells goods that were entrusted for transportation instead of delivering them to the intended destination.
A warehouse keeper intentionally misplaces high-value inventory and claims it was lost, then sells it privately.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Employee
description: Breach of trust by employee or agent misappropriating employer's property.
section_text: Section 408 of the Indian Penal Code deals with criminal breach of trust by an employee or an agent, involving the dishonestly misappropriating or converting property entrusted to them by their employer or principal.
example_scenarios: A company accountant transfers funds from the business account to their personal account without authorization.
A sales employee collects payments from customers but does not record them in the company books, keeping the money for personal use.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust by Public Servant
description: Breach of trust by public servant or person in position of trust.
section_text: Section 409 of the Indian Penal Code applies to criminal breach of trust by a public servant, or by someone who is in a position of trust for public duties.
legal_elements: Public servant or position of trust

punishment.imprisonment: Life imprisonment or up to 10 years

example_scenarios: A government officer responsible for welfare funds diverts the money to personal accounts instead of distributing it to beneficiaries.
A bank manager misuses depositors' funds that were entrusted to them in their official capacity for personal investments.

title: Stolen Property
description: Dishonest misappropriation or conversion of stolen property.
section_text: Section 410 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the dishonest misappropriation or conversion of stolen property. This provision applies when a person receives or keeps stolen property with the knowledge that it is stolen.
example_scenarios: A person buys a smartphone at an unusually low price from someone on the street, knowing it was likely stolen.
An individual accepts stolen jewelry from a friend who admits to having stolen it, and then attempts to sell it.

title: Receiving Stolen Property
description: Dishonestly receiving or retaining stolen property with knowledge.
section_text: Section 411 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of dishonestly receiving or retaining stolen property. It criminalizes receiving stolen goods, knowing that they have been stolen.
example_scenarios: A pawn shop owner knowingly accepts stolen electronic equipment from a known thief.
A person continues to keep a stolen bicycle after learning that it was taken without the owner's permission.

title: Receiving Property Stolen in Robbery
description: Receiving property known to be stolen from a robbery.
section_text: Section 412 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of receiving or retaining stolen property with knowledge that the property was stolen from a robbery.
example_scenarios: A person buys jewelry from someone who admits they obtained it through an armed robbery.
An individual accepts cash from a friend who confesses to having stolen it during a bank robbery.

title: Receiving Property Stolen in Dacoity
description: Receiving property known to be stolen through dacoity.
section_text: Section 413 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of receiving or retaining stolen property obtained through dacoity, i.e., a group robbery by armed persons.
legal_elements: Knowledge that property was obtained through dacoity
example_scenarios: A business owner purchases gold ornaments knowing they were stolen during a violent dacoity where five armed men robbed a jewelry store.
An individual accepts electronic goods from a group that admits to having obtained them through a highway dacoity.

title: Assisting in Concealing Stolen Property
description: Assisting in concealing or disposing of stolen property.
section_text: Section 414 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of assisting in concealing or disposing of stolen property, knowing it to be stolen.
example_scenarios: A friend helps hide stolen electronics in their garage knowing the items were taken without permission.
A shop owner helps alter the serial numbers on stolen motorcycles to make them harder to trace.

title: Cheating
description: Dishonestly inducing someone to deliver property or perform an act by deceit.
section_text: Section 415 of the Indian Penal Code defines the offence of cheating, which occurs when someone dishonestly induces another person to deliver property, money, or to do or omit something.
legal_elements: Delivery of property or performance of act
example_scenarios: A seller knowingly sells counterfeit goods as genuine products to unsuspecting customers.
A person convinces someone to invest in a fake business opportunity that doesn't exist.

title: Cheating by Personation
description: Cheating by pretending to be someone else to deceive others.
section_text: Section 416 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offence of cheating by pretending to be someone else to deceive others.
example_scenarios: A person pretends to be a government official to collect unauthorized payments from citizens.
An individual uses someone else's identity to obtain credit or loans in their name.

title: Punishment for Cheating
description: Punishment for cheating by dishonest inducement.
section_text: Section 417 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offence of cheating, specifically when someone dishonestly induces another person to deliver property, money, or to do or omit an act, with the intention of causing wrongful loss.
legal_elements: Delivery of property or performance of act
Intent to cause wrongful loss or gain
example_scenarios: A contractor takes advance payment for home repairs but intentionally uses substandard materials and does incomplete work.
A online seller accepts payment for goods but never ships them and stops responding to communications.

title: Cheating with Knowledge of Wrongful Loss
description: Cheating with knowledge that the act causes wrongful loss to another.
section_text: Section 418 of the Indian Penal Code punishes those who cheat by inducing someone to do something that results in wrongful loss, under circumstances where the act is done with the knowledge that they are deceiving the other person.
example_scenarios: A financial advisor knowingly recommends a failing investment to a client to earn commission, causing the client significant financial loss.
A car salesman sells a vehicle with known serious mechanical defects without disclosing them to the buyer.

title: Punishment for Cheating by Personation
description: Punishment for cheating by impersonation.
section_text: Section 419 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offence of cheating by impersonation, where a person cheats by pretending to be someone else.
example_scenarios: A person impersonates a bank manager to convince customers to transfer money to a fraudulent account.
An individual pretends to be a relative in need to solicit emergency funds from family members.

title: Cheating and Dishonestly Inducing Delivery of Property
description: Cheating by dishonestly inducing delivery of property causing wrongful loss.
section_text: Section 420 of the Indian Penal Code is a more severe form of cheating, as it specifically addresses situations where an individual cheats by dishonestly inducing another person to deliver property or perform an act that causes financial or property loss.
example_scenarios: A businessman induces investors to put money into a fake real estate project that doesn't exist, absconding with the funds.
A person sells a property they don't own to multiple buyers, collecting payments from each before disappearing.

title: Dishonestly Receiving Fraudulently Transferred Property
description: Criminalizes dishonestly receiving property that has been fraudulently transferred or altered.
section_text: Section 421 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of dishonestly receiving property that has been fraudulently transferred or altered.
legal_elements: Property must have been fraudulently transferred or altered
example_scenarios: A person knowingly buys a car at a suspiciously low price, later discovering its engine number was filed off and it was reported stolen.
A shop owner accepts jewelry for pawn, ignoring clear signs that the ownership documents are forged.

title: Dishonestly Removing Fraudulently Obtained Property
description: Punishes dishonestly removing or receiving property known to be fraudulently obtained.
section_text: Section 422 of the Indian Penal Code punishes the act of dishonestly removing a person's property, knowing that the property has been illegally obtained.
legal_elements: Knowledge that the property was fraudulently obtained
example_scenarios: A warehouse manager, aware that a shipment of electronics was acquired through a forged letter of credit, helps to move and hide the goods.
A person accepts a valuable painting from a friend, knowing it was stolen from a museum, and transports it to another city.

title: Fraudulent Transfer or False Representation of Property Value
description: Pertains to fraudulent transfer of property or falsely representing its value in a transaction.
section_text: Section 423 of the Indian Penal Code pertains to fraudulent transfer of property, particularly when someone intentionally makes a false representation of the value of a property in a transaction.
legal_elements: Transfer of property or false representation
False representation regarding the value of the property
example_scenarios: A seller conceals major structural defects in a house and provides a forged valuation certificate showing an inflated price to the buyer.
In a business sale, the owner significantly overstates the value of inventory and accounts receivable to secure a higher sale price.

title: Dishonest Removal or Concealment of Property
description: Addresses dishonest or fraudulent removal or concealment of property to deceive and cause wrongful loss.
section_text: Section 424 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offence of dishonest or fraudulent removal or concealment of property to deceive another person, especially in cases where the act leads to wrongful loss.
example_scenarios: A debtor, about to face court-ordered seizure of assets, secretly moves his expensive machinery to a relative's warehouse to hide it from creditors.
An employee, to cover up embezzlement, deletes digital records and physically removes ledger books containing evidence of the fraud.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Dishonest removal or concealment of property

title: Mischief - Intentional Damage to Property
description: Deals with mischief, specifically intentional destruction or damage of property causing harm to another.
section_text: Section 425 of the Indian Penal Code deals with mischief, specifically when a person intentionally destroys or damages property in a way that causes harm to someone else, without lawful excuse.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Causes or is likely to cause harm to any person
example_scenarios: In a fit of rage, a person throws a brick through the windshield of their neighbor's car during an argument.
A disgruntled former employee intentionally spills corrosive liquid on office computers, rendering them useless.

title: Punishment for Mischief
description: Punishment for mischief causing damage to property, such as breaking or defacing it.
section_text: Section 426 of the Indian Penal Code pertains to the punishment for mischief causing damage to property. It focuses on intentional harm done to someone's property, such as breaking or damaging it.
legal_elements: Act constitutes mischief as defined in IPC 425
example_scenarios: A person scratches the paint of a parked car with a key out of spite.
Someone breaks the windows of a vacant building for fun.

title: Mischief Causing Damage of Fifty Rupees or More
description: Addresses mischief causing damage to property valued at Rs. 50 or more.
section_text: Section 427 of the Indian Penal Code specifically addresses mischief that causes damage to property worth a certain amount, highlighting the severity based on the value of the loss caused.
legal_elements: Damage caused amounts to fifty rupees or more
example_scenarios: During a protest, a person smashes a shop's glass façade, causing damage estimated at Rs. 10,000.
An angry neighbor cuts down several mature fruit trees in an orchard, causing a loss of over Rs. 5,000.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Mischief causing damage of fifty rupees or more

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Animal
description: Deals with the crime of causing harm or death to an animal with the intention of causing cruelty or harm.
section_text: Section 428 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the crime of causing harm to animals. It penalizes acts of cruelty or harm inflicted on animals, particularly those that result in harm or death.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal
Value of the animal is ten rupees or more
example_scenarios: A person, out of malice, poisons a neighbor's pet dog valued at Rs. 15,000.
Someone intentionally runs over a stray calf with their vehicle, causing its death.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Mischief by killing or maiming animal

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Cattle, etc.
description: Penalizes killing, maiming, or rendering an animal useless, especially if it results in loss to the owner.
section_text: Section 429 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes acts of killing, maiming, or rendering an animal useless, especially if it results in loss to the owner of the animal.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any elephant, camel, horse, mule, buffalo, bull, cow or ox
Value of the animal is irrelevant for these specified animals
example_scenarios: A person, in a dispute over grazing land, intentionally shoots and kills a farmer's prized bullock.
Someone poisons a water trough, resulting in the death of several milch buffaloes belonging to a dairy farm.

title: Mischief by Injury to Works of Irrigation or Water Supply
description: Deals with mischief involving destruction or tampering with water supply for irrigation or drinking.
section_text: Section 430 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the crime of mischief involving the destruction or tampering with the water supply, which could result in the damage or obstruction of water meant for irrigation or drinking purposes.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Causing diminution of water supply for agriculture or for human consumption
Or injury to works of irrigation or to works for water supply
example_scenarios: A farmer, to divert more water to his own fields, deliberately breaks a canal gate, disrupting the water supply to downstream villages.
A construction company illegally diverts a pipeline, severely reducing drinking water supply to a residential colony.

title: Mischief by Injury to Public Road, Bridge, etc.
description: Addresses destruction or damage of public property like railways, bridges, roads, or other infrastructure.
section_text: Section 431 of the Indian Penal Code specifically addresses the destruction or damage of public property like railways, bridges, roads, or other forms of infrastructure. It targets intentional harm to vital public systems and utilities.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Causing destruction or damage to any public road, bridge, navigable river or channel
Or renders such road, bridge, river or channel impassable or less safe for traveling or conveying property
example_scenarios: Protestors dig a trench across a national highway, making it impassable for traffic.
A person removes critical bolts from a pedestrian footbridge, endangering public safety.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Mischief to public road, bridge, etc.

title: Mischief by Causing Inundation or Obstruction
description: Addresses mischief causing damage to public property, particularly irrigation systems and water channels.
section_text: Section 432 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the punishment for mischief causing damage to public property, including destruction of irrigation systems and water channels.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Causing inundation or obstruction to any public drainage
Or injury to any such works
example_scenarios: A landowner, to prevent waterlogging on his plot, illegally blocks a major public stormwater drain, causing flooding in a low-lying residential area.
A contractor illegally dumps construction debris into a canal, obstructing the flow and causing water to overflow onto farmland.

title: Mischief by Destroying or Moving Landmark
description: Applies to destruction or damage of public infrastructure with intention of obstructing or disrupting services.
section_text: Section 433 of the Indian Penal Code applies to the destruction or damage of public infrastructure, particularly with the intention of obstructing or disrupting the normal functioning of services, including transportation, utilities, or communication systems.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Destroys or moves any landmark fixed by public authority
Or does any act to diminish its utility or value as a landmark
example_scenarios: A person, to create a land dispute, removes the concrete boundary stone marking the edge of a government forest reserve.
A builder, to illegally extend his construction, destroys the official survey marker placed by the revenue department.

title: Mischief by Destroying Landmark of a Place of Worship
description: Addresses mischief involving damaging or destroying property of another, especially land, building, or goods.
section_text: Section 434 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the mischief that involves damaging or destroying the property of another, especially in cases where the damage is caused to land, building, or goods.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Destroys or moves any landmark of a place of worship or of a place used as a place of sepulture
example_scenarios: During a communal dispute, a person destroys the stone marking the boundary of a mosque's property.
Someone removes the ancient boundary marker of a Hindu temple's land to encroach upon it.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Mischief to landmark of place of worship

title: Mischief by Fire or Explosive with Intent to Cause Damage
description: Punishes mischief causing damage to property with a value exceeding ₹50, by fire or explosive.
section_text: Section 435 of the Indian Penal Code punishes mischief causing damage to property with a value exceeding ₹50. This law is designed to deal with damage that results in significant financial loss.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance
Damage caused amounts to one hundred rupees or more (or in case of agricultural produce, ten rupees or more)
example_scenarios: A person sets fire to a rival's crop storage shed, destroying produce worth Rs. 50,000.
Someone throws a crude explosive device at a shopfront, causing damage estimated at Rs. 25,000.

title: Mischief by Fire or Explosive with Intent to Destroy House, etc.
description: Deals with mischief by fire or explosive substances likely to cause damage to property or human life.
section_text: Section 436 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the crime of mischief by fire or explosive substances, which is likely to cause damage to property or human life.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Commits mischief by fire or any explosive substance
Intending to cause, or knowing it to be likely to cause, the destruction of any building used as a human dwelling, or as a place for custody of property
example_scenarios: A person, seeking revenge, sets fire to their former employer's house while knowing the family is away.
A gang uses a petrol bomb to destroy a warehouse where goods are stored, endangering the security guard's quarters attached to it.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Mischief by fire to dwelling, etc.

title: Mischief with Intent to Destroy or Make Unsafe a Vessel
description: Penalizes destruction of property by fire, with specific intent to cause damage or endanger lives.
section_text: Section 437 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes the destruction of property by fire, with the specific intent to cause damage to the property or to endanger lives.
legal_elements: Intention to cause, or knowledge that the act will cause, wrongful loss or damage
Commits mischief to any decked vessel or any vessel of twenty tons burden or upwards
Intending to destroy or render unsafe that vessel
example_scenarios: A disgruntled crew member intentionally starts a fire in the engine room of a cargo ship to destroy it.
A person, for insurance fraud, sets fire to his own fishing trawler (over 20 tons) while it is docked.

title: Punishment for Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking
description: Deals with the offense of granting anticipatory bail in cases where the accused is likely to face arrest due to a non-bailable offence.
section_text: Section 438 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of granting anticipatory bail in cases where the accused is likely to face arrest due to a non-bailable offence.

title: Special Power of High Court or Court of Session Regarding Bail
description: Provides the legal provision for granting anticipatory bail to individuals charged with a non-bailable offence, prior to their arrest.
section_text: Section 439 of the Indian Penal Code provides the legal provision for granting anticipatory bail to individuals charged with a non-bailable offence, prior to their arrest.

title: Mischief Committed After Preparation Made for Causing Death or Hurt
description: Addresses mischief by fire or explosive substances, causing significant damage to property or endangering lives.
section_text: Section 440 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the crime of mischief by fire or explosive substances, causing significant damage to property or endangering lives.
legal_elements: Having made preparation for causing to any person death, or hurt, or wrongful restraint, or fear of death, hurt, or wrongful restraint
example_scenarios: A person, after arming himself with a weapon to confront anyone who tries to stop him, sets fire to his business partner's office.
A group, having prepared to assault security guards, uses explosives to blow up a rival's construction site gate.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Mischief after preparation for causing hurt, etc.

title: Criminal Trespass
description: Defines and punishes the offense of criminal trespass, i.e., entering or remaining on someone’s property without permission.
section_text: Section 441 of the Indian Penal Code defines and punishes the offense of criminal trespass, i.e., entering or remaining on someone’s property without permission.
legal_elements: Entry into or upon property in possession of another
If such entry is lawful, unlawfully remaining there
With intent to commit an offence or to intimidate, insult or annoy the person in possession
example_scenarios: A person jumps over a fence into a private garden to take a shortcut, annoying the homeowner.
After a public event ends, a person refuses to leave the privately-owned stadium grounds despite being asked to do so.

title: House-Trespass
description: Deals with criminal trespass into a dwelling house or a place of worship, with more severe consequences.
section_text: Section 442 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of criminal trespass into a dwelling house or a place of worship, with more severe consequences due to the sensitive nature of these locations.
legal_elements: By entering into or remaining in any building, tent or vessel used as a human dwelling, or any building used as a place for worship, or as a place for the custody of property
example_scenarios: A person sneaks into a neighbor's house through an open window to retrieve a borrowed item without permission, intending to surprise/annoy them.
A group enters a closed temple at night without authorization, causing annoyance to the caretaker.

title: Lurking House-Trespass
description: Deals with the act of committing mischief by trespassing on a property, damaging or destroying it.
section_text: Section 443 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of committing mischief by trespassing on a property, damaging or destroying it.
legal_elements: Having taken precautions to conceal such house-trespass from some person who has a right to exclude or eject the trespasser
example_scenarios: A person waits until nightfall and wears dark clothes to sneak into a factory compound unseen by guards, intending to commit mischief inside.
Someone hides in the bushes outside a house, waiting for the occupants to leave before entering through a back window.

title: Lurking House-Trespass by Night
description: Concerned with mischief by causing damage to the property by means of fire or explosive substances.
section_text: Section 444 of the Indian Penal Code is concerned with the offense of mischief by causing damage to the property by means of fire or explosive substances.
example_scenarios: A thief conceals himself and enters a house through a window at 2 AM to steal jewelry.
A person sneaks into an office building after hours, hiding from the night watchman.

title: House-Breaking
description: Pertains to mischief by fire or explosive substance, causing damage to property.
section_text: Section 445 of the Indian Penal Code pertains to the offense of mischief by fire or explosive substance, causing damage to property.
legal_elements: By effecting entrance into a house or any part of it, or by quitting the house or any part of it
In any of the six ways described in the section (e.g., through a passage made by oneself, by unlocking, etc.)
example_scenarios: A person uses a crowbar to pry open a locked window to enter a house.
A trespasser unlocks a door using a stolen key to gain entry into an apartment.

title: House-Breaking by Night
description: Deals with mischief causing damage to property through an explosive substance.
section_text: Section 446 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of mischief causing damage to property through an explosive substance.
example_scenarios: A burglar cuts through a window grill at 3 AM to enter a house and steal valuables.
A person uses a duplicate key to unlock and enter a shop after midnight.

title: Punishment for Criminal Trespass
description: Punishes trespassing onto someone else’s land or property.
section_text: Section 447 of the Indian Penal Code punishes trespassing onto someone else’s land or property.
legal_elements: Commits criminal trespass as defined in IPC 441
example_scenarios: A person is found camping on private farmland without the owner's permission, refusing to leave when asked.
Someone enters a gated community by jumping the fence to meet a friend, against the society's rules.

title: Punishment for House-Trespass
description: Deals with the offense of house trespass, which involves entering or remaining unlawfully in someone's home or residence.
section_text: Section 448 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of house trespass, which involves entering or remaining unlawfully in someone's home or residence.
legal_elements: Commits house-trespass as defined in IPC 442
example_scenarios: A person enters a neighbor's unlocked house without permission to use the bathroom, causing annoyance.
An ex-employee refuses to leave the company's staff quarters after his employment is terminated and he is asked to vacate.

title: House-Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Death
description: Penalizes house trespass committed with an intention to commit a serious crime.
section_text: Section 449 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes house trespass committed with an intention to commit a serious crime.
legal_elements: In order to commit an offence punishable with death
example_scenarios: A person breaks into a house with the specific plan to murder the occupant.
Assassins trespass into a political leader's residence with the intent to assassinate him.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: House-trespass to commit offence punishable with death

title: House-Trespass in Order to Commit Offence Punishable with Imprisonment for Life
description: Deals with house trespass committed with the intent to commit a crime punishable with life imprisonment or a term of 7 years or more.
section_text: Section 450 of the Indian Penal Code deals with house trespass committed with the intent to commit a crime punishable with life imprisonment or a term of 7 years or more.
legal_elements: In order to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment for life, or with imprisonment for a term which may extend to ten years

punishment.imprisonment: imprisonment for life, or up to 10 years and fine

example_scenarios: A gang breaks into a bank manager's house to kidnap him for ransom, an offence punishable with life imprisonment.
A person trespasses into a house intending to cause grievous hurt with a dangerous weapon to the occupant.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: House-trespass to commit offence punishable with life imprisonment

title: House-trespass with criminal intent
description: Punishment for house-trespass with intent to commit a criminal offense.
section_text: Section 451 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for the offense of house-trespass, specifically when it involves the intention to commit a criminal offense.
legal_elements: Entering or remaining in a building or property
Intent to commit a criminal offense
example_scenarios: A person enters a closed shop at night intending to steal cash from the register.
An individual remains in an office building after hours with the intent to access confidential files illegally.

title: House-trespass with intent to commit violence
description: Punishment for house-trespass with intent to commit violent acts or cause harm.
section_text: Section 452 of the Indian Penal Code provides the punishment for house-trespass when the offender intends to commit a violent act or threatens harm while committing the offense.
legal_elements: Entering or remaining in a building
Intent to commit violence or cause harm
example_scenarios: A person breaks into a home intending to assault the occupants during a dispute.
An individual enters an office with the intent to damage property and threaten employees.

title: Mischief damaging public property
description: Punishment for mischief by damaging public property or property used for public purposes.
section_text: Section 453 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for committing mischief by damaging property that belongs to someone else and which is used for public purposes.
example_scenarios: A person deliberately damages public benches in a park.
An individual breaks windows at a public library causing property damage.

title: House-breaking with criminal intent
description: Punishment for house-breaking with intent to commit a crime.
section_text: Section 454 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for house-breaking with the intent to commit a crime.
legal_elements: Breaking into a building or property
Intent to commit theft, assault or other criminal act
example_scenarios: A person breaks a window to enter a home with the intent to steal electronics.
An individual forces open a door to a business to commit theft after hours.

title: House-breaking by night with criminal intent
description: Punishment for house-breaking during night with intent to commit offense.
section_text: Section 455 of the Indian Penal Code relates to punishment for house-breaking by night, with the intent to commit an offense.
example_scenarios: A person breaks into a home at 2 AM intending to steal jewelry.
An individual forces entry into a store after midnight to commit theft.

title: Unlawful entry with criminal intent
description: Punishment for unlawful entry or breaking into property with criminal intent.
section_text: Section 456 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes those involved in house-breaking or unlawful entry with the intent to commit any offense, including theft or assault.
legal_elements: Unlawful entry or breaking into property
example_scenarios: A person enters a construction site without permission intending to steal tools.
An individual climbs over a fence into a property with intent to vandalize.

title: Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night
description: Punishes the act of committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking between sunset and sunrise.
section_text: Section 457 of the Indian Penal Code punishes the act of committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking between sunset and sunrise.
legal_elements: The act is done by night (between sunset and sunrise)
example_scenarios: A thief climbs through a window into a house after dark to steal jewelry.
A person hides inside a commercial building before it closes and then attempts to rob the safe at night.

title: Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking by Night after Preparation for Hurt, etc.
description: Punishes lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night when prepared to cause hurt, assault, or wrongful restraint.
section_text: Section 458 of the Indian Penal Code punishes lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night when prepared to cause hurt, assault, or wrongful restraint.
legal_elements: Committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking by night
example_scenarios: A burglar enters a home at night carrying a knife, prepared to use it if confronted by the residents.
A group breaks into a warehouse after dark, armed with clubs, ready to assault any security guard they encounter.

title: Grievous Hurt caused whilst Committing Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking
description: Punishes the act of causing grievous hurt to any person whilst committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking.
section_text: Section 459 of the Indian Penal Code punishes the act of causing grievous hurt to any person whilst committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking.
legal_elements: Causing grievous hurt to any person
The hurt is caused during the commission of the trespass/breaking
example_scenarios: A burglar breaks into a house and, upon being discovered, attacks the homeowner, breaking their arm.
During a nighttime robbery, a trespasser strikes a security guard on the head with a blunt object, causing a severe fracture.

title: All Persons Jointly Engaged in Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking when Grievous Hurt caused
description: Specifies that if grievous hurt is caused by one person during a joint lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, all participants are guilty.
section_text: Section 460 of the Indian Penal Code specifies that if grievous hurt is caused by one person during a joint lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, all participants are guilty.
legal_elements: Two or more persons are jointly committing lurking house-trespass or house-breaking
One of them commits grievous hurt
All are deemed guilty of the offence under section 459
example_scenarios: Two people break into a shop together. One attacks and seriously injures the night watchman. Both are charged under this section.
A group commits a home invasion. While one member is stealing, another assaults the homeowner. All members of the group are held responsible for the assault.

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Joint Liability for Burglary with Grievous Hurt

title: Mischief damaging valuable property
description: Punishment for mischief by damaging valuable property with deliberate harm intent.
section_text: Section 461 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of committing mischief by the destruction of a valuable property or by the act of damaging it with a deliberate intention to harm.
example_scenarios: A person deliberately sets fire to a valuable antique car.
An individual intentionally destroys expensive equipment in a laboratory.

title: Damage to property with explosives
description: Punishment for causing damage to property using explosive substances.
section_text: Section 462 of the Indian Penal Code covers the act of causing damage to a property with an explosive substance.
example_scenarios: A person uses dynamite to damage a building during a protest.
An individual sets off an explosive device to destroy a bridge.

title: Forgery of documents

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for making false documents with intent to deceive.
section_text: Section 463 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offense of forgery, which involves making a false document with an intention to deceive.
example_scenarios: A person creates a fake university degree to obtain employment.
An individual alters a check amount to receive more money than authorized.

title: Making false documents or parts

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for making false documents or parts of documents with intent to deceive.
section_text: Section 464 of the Indian Penal Code defines and penalizes making a false document or part of a document with the intent to deceive, following the broader offense of forgery under Section 463.
legal_elements: Making false document or part of document
example_scenarios: A person alters the date on a contract to make it appear more recent.
An individual changes the beneficiary name on a will to divert inheritance.

title: Forgery of documents with intent to harm or defraud

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for making false documents or parts with intent to defraud or harm rights
section_text: Section 465 of the Indian Penal Code prescribes punishment for forgery, which includes making a false document or part of a document with the intent to cause harm or defraud.
legal_elements: Making a false document or part of a document
Intent to defraud or harm the rights of another
example_scenarios: Creating a fake property deed to illegally transfer ownership of land
Altering a contract to change payment terms to defraud a business partner

title: Forgery of documents, seals or electronic records

chapter.chapter_name: Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for forging documents, seals or electronic records with fraudulent intent
section_text: Section 466 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of forgery of documents, seals, or electronic records with the intent to commit fraud or deceit.
legal_elements: Forging a document, seal, or electronic record
example_scenarios: Creating a fake government seal on a document to make it appear official
Tampering with electronic court records to alter case outcomes

title: Forgery of valuable security or will

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for forgery of valuable securities, wills or important documents
section_text: Section 467 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the forgery of valuable security, will, or other important documents that can cause severe harm to the victim.
legal_elements: Forgery of valuable security, will, or significant legal document
example_scenarios: Creating a fake stock certificate to fraudulently claim ownership of shares
Forging a will to illegally inherit property from a deceased person

title: Forgery with intent to cheat or defraud

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for forgery committed with intent to cheat or defraud
section_text: Section 468 of the Indian Penal Code targets forgery committed with the intention to cheat or defraud someone.
legal_elements: Intent to cheat or defraud a person or organization
example_scenarios: Creating fake invoices to cheat a company out of payment for services never rendered
Forging academic transcripts to fraudulently gain admission to a university

title: Forgery to harm reputation

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for forgery intended to damage a person's reputation
section_text: Section 469 of the Indian Penal Code targets forgery with the intention of harming a person's reputation.
legal_elements: Intent to harm someone's reputation through deceitful or fraudulent means
example_scenarios: Creating fake social media posts attributed to someone to damage their public image
Forging a letter that appears to be from a professional making false allegations about a colleague

title: Using forged document to commit fraud

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using forged document with intent to defraud
section_text: Section 470 of the Indian Penal Code provides for the offense of using a forged document to commit fraud, even when the document is not yet passed as genuine.
example_scenarios: Presenting a forged income certificate to obtain a bank loan
Using a fake identity document to enter a restricted area

title: Using forged document knowing it is false

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using forged document with knowledge of its falsity
section_text: Section 471 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the use of forged documents, which involves using a document knowing it is forged or altered.
example_scenarios: A person uses a counterfeit passport knowing it is fake to travel internationally
An employee submits forged educational certificates to an employer knowing they are not genuine

title: Making or possessing forged documents for fraud

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for making or possessing forged documents with fraudulent intent
section_text: Section 472 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of making or possessing forged documents with the intent to use them in a fraudulent manner.
legal_elements: Making, possessing, or having custody of forged documents
Intent to use them for fraudulent purposes
example_scenarios: A person creates fake vaccination certificates with intent to sell them
An individual keeps counterfeit currency notes with plans to use them in transactions

title: Using forged documents or goods as genuine

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using forged documents or goods with intent to pass as genuine
section_text: Section 473 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the use of forged documents or goods for the purpose of passing them as genuine with the intent to defraud.
legal_elements: Intent to pass it off as genuine
example_scenarios: Selling counterfeit branded watches as authentic luxury items
Presenting a forged university certificate as genuine to secure a job

title: Possessing or using forged documents with knowledge

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for possessing or using forged documents knowing them to be forged
section_text: Section 474 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes individuals who possess or use forged documents knowing them to be forged and with the intention of using them for fraudulent purposes.
example_scenarios: Keeping counterfeit currency notes knowing they are fake with intent to use them
Holding a forged driver's license knowing it is fake to use for identification

title: Possessing forged documents for fraudulent use

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for possessing forged documents with intent to use for fraud
section_text: Section 475 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the possession of forged documents with the intent to use them for committing fraud or furthering a dishonest purpose.
legal_elements: Intent to use them for fraud or dishonest purposes
example_scenarios: Keeping fake property papers with plans to sell non-existent land
Possessing forged medical certificates to make false insurance claims

crime_type_mapping.crime_name: Possession of forged documents for fraud

title: Falsifying or altering documents or public registers

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for falsifying or altering documents or public registers
section_text: Section 476 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes the act of falsifying or altering any document, record, or public register in such a way as to make it appear as if it has been executed or registered in a legitimate manner.
legal_elements: Falsifying or altering document or public register
Making it appear legitimately executed or registered
example_scenarios: Altering birth records to change a person's date of birth for benefits
Falsifying land registry entries to show false ownership

title: Falsifying accounts or financial records

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for falsifying accounts or books of accounts
section_text: Section 477 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of falsifying accounts or books of accounts to conceal financial wrongdoing or misrepresent an individual's financial situation.
legal_elements: Falsifying financial records, accounts, or books
example_scenarios: An accountant alters company books to hide embezzlement
A business owner creates fake invoices to show inflated revenue to investors

title: Using forged documents or falsified records

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using forged documents or falsified records to deceive or cheat
section_text: Section 478 of the Indian Penal Code covers the offense of using forged documents or falsified records to deceive or cheat.
legal_elements: Using forged documents or falsified records
example_scenarios: Submitting falsified medical records to an insurance company to claim benefits
Using forged academic records to apply for a government scholarship

title: Using false tokens, seals, or marks

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for using false tokens, seals, or marks to deceive or cheat
section_text: Section 479 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the use of false tokens, seals, or marks to deceive or cheat others.
legal_elements: Using false tokens, seals, or marks
Intent to commit fraud or deceive
example_scenarios: Using a counterfeit government seal on a document to give it false authority
Employing fake security tokens to gain unauthorized access to a restricted facility

title: Forgery of public documents or stamps

chapter.chapter_name: Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks

description: Punishment for forgery of public documents or official stamps
section_text: Section 480 of the Indian Penal Code punishes the act of forgery in public documents or stamps that are used for official purposes.
legal_elements: Forgery of public documents or stamps
example_scenarios: Creating fake passport stamps to show false entry/exit records
Forging court documents to make them appear as official judgments

title: Using Forged Documents in Business or Public Transactions

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Penalizes using forged documents, seals, or stamps for fraudulent purposes in business or public transactions.
section_text: Section 481 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the offense of using a forged document, seal, or stamp in the course of business or public transactions.
legal_elements: Using a forged document, seal, or stamp
Use occurs in the course of business or public transactions
example_scenarios: A businessman submits a forged bank guarantee seal to secure a government contract.
An individual uses a forged property deed stamp to illegally transfer ownership of land.

title: False Accusation of Forgery

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Protects individuals from false accusations of forgery and allows legal action against false accusers.
section_text: Section 482 of the Indian Penal Code protects individuals who are wrongfully accused of forgery by allowing them to take action against false allegations of forgery.
legal_elements: Intent to harm reputation or cause legal issues
example_scenarios: An employee falsely accuses a colleague of forging a signature on an official document to get them fired.
A person files a police complaint alleging a neighbor forged a will, knowing the allegation to be false.

title: Using Forged Documents for Fraudulent Transactions

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Addresses the offense of using forged or altered documents with intent to commit fraud or deceit.
section_text: Section 483 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offense of using forged or altered documents for fraudulent transactions.
legal_elements: Using a forged or altered document
Intent to commit fraud or deceit
example_scenarios: A person uses a forged academic certificate to secure a job.
An individual submits an altered sales invoice to claim a higher insurance payout.

title: Possession of Forged Documents for Fraudulent Use

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Deals with possessing forged documents with the intent to use them for fraudulent transactions.
section_text: Section 484 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of having possession of forged documents with the intention of using them for fraudulent purposes.
legal_elements: Intent to use it for fraudulent purposes
example_scenarios: Police find a stack of forged property deeds in a person's home during a raid.
An individual is caught at an airport with a forged passport they intended to use for illegal immigration.

title: Making or Using Forged Seals

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Penalizes making, possessing, or using forged seals with intent to deceive or defraud.
section_text: Section 485 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of making or using false or forged seals, which can lead to fraudulent activities.
legal_elements: Making, possessing, or using a forged seal
example_scenarios: A person creates a fake notary public seal to authenticate fraudulent property documents.
A company uses a forged government approval seal on a product to bypass regulations.

title: Possession of Counterfeit or Forged Stamps

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Criminalizes possessing counterfeit or forged stamps with intent to use them for deception or fraud.
section_text: Section 486 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the act of possessing a counterfeit or forged stamp that is used for fraudulent purposes.
legal_elements: Possession of a counterfeit or forged stamp
Intent to use for deception or fraud
example_scenarios: A shopkeeper is found with a batch of fake revenue stamps to avoid paying government duty.
An individual possesses forged postage stamps to mail packages illegally.

title: Making or Possessing Counterfeit Currency

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Deals with making, possessing, or distributing counterfeit coins or currency notes with intent to defraud.
section_text: Section 487 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of making or possessing a counterfeit coin or currency note with the intent to use them for fraud.
legal_elements: Making, possessing, or distributing counterfeit coin or currency note
example_scenarios: A person is caught printing fake 500 rupee notes in a hidden press.
An individual is found with a bag of counterfeit coins intended to be used in vending machines.

title: Possession of Counterfeit Valuable Security

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Penalizes possessing counterfeit valuable securities with intent to use them for fraud.
section_text: Section 488 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of possessing counterfeit or forged valuable security, with the intention to defraud others.
example_scenarios: A person holds forged government bonds to use as collateral for a bank loan.
An individual is found with counterfeit stock certificates to sell to unsuspecting investors.

title: Possession or Use of Counterfeit Currency or Forged Stamps

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Focuses on possession, use, or distribution of counterfeit currency or forged stamps with fraudulent intent.
section_text: Section 489 of the Indian Penal Code focuses on the possession, use, or distribution of counterfeit or forged currency notes or stamps with the intent to commit fraud.
legal_elements: Possessing, using, or distributing counterfeit currency or forged stamps
example_scenarios: A person is arrested while trying to deposit a large amount of counterfeit notes into a bank account.
A gang is caught selling forged postage stamps and fake currency notes in the black market.

title: Making or Possessing Forged Documents for Fraud

chapter.chapter_name: Offences relating to Documents and to Property Marks

description: Criminalizes making, possessing, or using forged documents with intent to deceive or defraud.
section_text: Section 490 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of making or possessing counterfeit documents or forged items with the intent to use them for fraudulent purposes.
legal_elements: Making, possessing, or using forged documents
example_scenarios: A person creates a fake driving license to rent a car under a false identity.
An employee is found with a forged medical certificate to justify prolonged absence from work.

title: Wrongful Detention
description: Penalizes wrongful detention of a person in a place without legal authority or consent.
section_text: Section 491 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes the act of wrongfully detaining a person, especially in the case of an individual being confined in a place with no legal justification.
example_scenarios: A landlord locks a tenant inside a room over a rent dispute.
A shopkeeper detains a customer suspected of shoplifting without calling the police.

title: Wrongful Detention for Extortion or Ransom
description: Deals with unlawfully detaining a person with intent to extort money, force an illegal act, or secure a ransom.
section_text: Section 492 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the act of detaining a person with the intent to extort or force them into an illegal act or demand a ransom.
legal_elements: Intent to extort money, force an illegal act, or secure a ransom
example_scenarios: A person is kidnapped and held in a secluded location until a ransom is paid by the family.
A businessman is forcibly detained and threatened to sign over his company shares.

title: Coercion or Deceit into Marriage
description: Concerns coercing or deceiving someone into marriage by false promises or fraud.
section_text: Section 493 of the Indian Penal Code concerns the act of coercing someone into marriage by deceit or fraudulent means, particularly through misrepresentation of facts.
legal_elements: Coercion or deception of a person
example_scenarios: A man falsely promises a woman a job abroad to convince her to marry him, with no intention of fulfilling the promise.
A person conceals a serious contagious disease to induce another into marriage.

title: Bigamy
description: Criminalizes marrying another person while already married, without the consent of the first spouse.
section_text: Section 494 of the Indian Penal Code criminalizes the act of marrying another person while already being married to someone else, without the consent of the first spouse.
legal_elements: Without the consent of the first spouse
example_scenarios: A man, already legally married, conducts a marriage ceremony with another woman without divorcing his first wife.
A woman, whose husband is missing, remarries without obtaining a decree of presumption of death and divorce.

title: Concealing Prior Marriage to Commit Fraud
description: Penalizes concealing the fact of a previous marriage while entering into another marriage, committing fraud against the new spouse.
section_text: Section 495 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes the act of concealing the fact of a previous marriage while entering into another marriage, thus committing fraud against the new spouse.
example_scenarios: A man hides his existing marriage from his fiancée and marries her, leading her to believe she is his first wife.
A woman obtains a marriage certificate by declaring herself unmarried, knowing she is still legally married to another person.

title: Marriage by Concealment of Prior Spouse
description: Punishes marrying a woman without informing her of a prior marriage, committing fraud against her.
section_text: Section 496 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for marrying a woman without informing her that he has a prior spouse, and thus committing fraud by concealing a fact.
legal_elements: Without informing her of a prior marriage
example_scenarios: A man, already married, woos and marries a woman without ever revealing his existing wife and children.
A person obtains a second marriage certificate by presenting himself as a bachelor, defrauding the second wife.

title: Adultery
description: Addresses adultery, where a man has sexual intercourse with a married woman without the consent of her husband.
section_text: Section 497 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offense of adultery, where a man has sexual intercourse with a woman who is married to another man, without the consent of the husband.
legal_elements: A man has sexual intercourse with a woman
The woman is married to another man
Without the consent of her husband
example_scenarios: A man engages in a consensual sexual relationship with a married woman, whose husband is unaware and does not consent.
A married woman has an affair, and her lover is charged under this section.

title: Cruelty by Husband or Relatives
description: Addresses cruelty by a husband or his relatives towards the wife, including physical or mental harm.
section_text: Section 498 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offense of cruelty by a husband or his relatives towards the wife, which includes physical or mental harm.
legal_elements: Cruelty committed by husband or his relatives
example_scenarios: A husband and his mother constantly verbally abuse and threaten the wife for not bringing enough dowry.
A wife is subjected to physical beatings by her husband for minor disagreements.

title: Defamation
description: Defines the offense of defamation, harming another’s reputation through false statements with malicious intent.
section_text: Section 499 of the Indian Penal Code defines the offense of defamation, which occurs when an individual intentionally harms another’s reputation through false statements.
example_scenarios: A person spreads a false rumor that a local businessman is involved in illegal activities, damaging his clientele.
An employee falsely tells colleagues that their manager has been stealing from the company.

title: Punishment for Defamation
description: Prescribes punishment for defamation, making it a criminal offense.
section_text: Section 500 of the Indian Penal Code prescribes the punishment for defamation. It makes the act of defaming another individual a criminal offense.
example_scenarios: After being found guilty of defamation under Section 499, the accused is sentenced to imprisonment or fine under this section.
A journalist is convicted and punished for publishing a false story that damaged a politician's reputation.

title: Defamation by Printing or Engraving
description: Deals with punishment for defamation by printing or engraving a false statement that damages reputation.
section_text: Section 501 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for defamation by printing or engraving a false statement that damages someone's reputation.
legal_elements: Printing or engraving a false statement
example_scenarios: A newspaper publishes an article with false allegations of corruption against a public official.
A pamphlet is printed and distributed accusing a doctor of malpractice without evidence.

title: Sale or Possession of Defamatory Material
description: Concerns punishment for sale or possession of printed or engraved material with intent to defame.
section_text: Section 502 of the Indian Penal Code concerns the punishment for the sale or possession of printed or engraved material with intent to defame someone.
example_scenarios: A person is found selling pamphlets containing false and damaging statements about a local business owner.
A bookshop owner stocks and sells a book known to contain defamatory chapters about a historical figure.

title: Criminal Intimidation

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Deals with punishment for criminal intimidation, threatening another with injury to force them to act against their will.
section_text: Section 503 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for criminal intimidation, where a person threatens another with injury or harm to force them to do something.
legal_elements: Intent to force them to act against their will
example_scenarios: A loan shark threatens to break a debtor's legs if the loan is not repaid by the end of the week.
An employer threatens an employee with physical harm if they report workplace safety violations.

title: Intentional Insult to Provoke Breach of Peace

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Addresses intentional insult with intent to provoke a breach of peace.
section_text: Section 504 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offense of intentional insult with the intent to provoke a breach of peace.
legal_elements: Intent to provoke breach of peace or commit a crime
example_scenarios: During a heated argument, a person uses deeply offensive caste-based slurs intending to make the other person attack them.
In a public gathering, an individual insults a community leader with the aim of inciting a riot.

title: Statements Creating Public Fear or Alarm

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Deals with statements made with intention to create fear or alarm among the public, leading to potential disorder.
section_text: Section 505 of the Indian Penal Code deals with statements made with the intention to create fear or alarm among the public, leading to potential public disorder.
legal_elements: Intention to create fear, alarm, or potential public disorder
example_scenarios: A person spreads a false rumor on social media that a terrorist attack is imminent in a city, causing panic.
During a religious festival, an individual announces a fake bomb threat to disrupt the celebrations.

title: Punishment for Criminal Intimidation

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Deals with punishment for criminal intimidation where a person threatens to cause harm or injury.
section_text: Section 506 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for criminal intimidation where a person threatens to cause harm or injury to another person.
legal_elements: Threatening to cause harm or injury
Intent to induce the person to act against their will

punishment.imprisonment: up to 2 years or up to 7 years if threat is to cause death/grievous hurt

example_scenarios: A person threatens to burn down a shop if the owner does not pay protection money.
During a dispute, one party threatens to kill the other if they file a police complaint.

title: Criminal Intimidation by Anonymous Communication

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Addresses criminal intimidation by sending an anonymous letter or communication threatening harm.
section_text: Section 507 of the Indian Penal Code addresses the offense of criminal intimidation by anonymous communication.
legal_elements: Committing the intimidation by anonymous communication
example_scenarios: A person receives a letter with no return address threatening to reveal personal secrets unless money is paid.
Anonymous emails are sent to a public figure threatening violence if they do not resign.

title: Intimidation to Compel Action

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Penalizes threatening to harm someone to force them to act in a certain way.
section_text: Section 508 of the Indian Penal Code penalizes anyone who threatens to harm or injure someone in order to force them to act in a certain way, with an intention to compel them to do something they do not want to.
legal_elements: Intent to force someone to act in a certain way against their will
example_scenarios: A person threatens to harm a witness's family to force them to give false testimony in court.
An ex-partner threatens to publish private photos unless the victim withdraws a legal complaint.

title: Insulting Modesty of a Woman

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Deals with punishment for using words, gestures, or acts to insult the modesty of a woman.
section_text: Section 509 of the Indian Penal Code deals with the punishment for using words, gestures, or acts to insult the modesty of a woman.
legal_elements: Insulting the modesty of a woman
example_scenarios: A man makes lewd comments and whistles at a woman walking on the street.
A person makes an obscene gesture towards a female colleague in the office.

title: Indecent Act in Public

chapter.chapter_name: Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult and Annoyance

description: Punishes individuals who commit indecent acts in public places.
section_text: Section 510 of the Indian Penal Code punishes individuals who commit indecent acts in public places.
example_scenarios: A person is found urinating on a public street in full view of others.
An intoxicated individual is causing a disturbance by shouting obscenities in a public park.

title: Punishment for Attempting to Commit Offence
description: Provides punishment for attempting to commit an offense punishable with imprisonment, even if the attempt fails.
section_text: Section 511 of the Indian Penal Code provides punishment for attempting to commit an offense that is punishable with imprisonment, but the attempt fails.
legal_elements: The offense is punishable by imprisonment

punishment.imprisonment: up to half the term prescribed for the completed offense
punishment.triable_by: Court by which the offence attempted is triable

example_scenarios: A person breaks into a house with intent to steal but is caught by police before taking anything.
An individual tries to administer poison to another but is stopped before the victim consumes it.
